questions.prompt: Positively charged subatomic particle found in the nucleus; its count defines the element's atomic number.
questions.explanation: The proton was identified by Ernest Rutherford in 1917 through experiments bombarding nitrogen gas with alpha particles, producing hydrogen nuclei. Carrying a +1 charge and a mass of approximately 1 atomic mass unit (amu), protons sit in the nucleus alongside neutrons. On the NYS Chemistry Reference Tables, the atomic number printed in each Periodic Table box equals the number of protons—a neutral atom of carbon (Z=6) has exactly 6 protons. Regents items often pair this with mass number to ask students to calculate neutrons (mass number − atomic number).

questions.prompt: Subatomic particle with no charge and a mass of about 1 amu, located in the nucleus.
questions.explanation: James Chadwick discovered the neutron in 1932 by analyzing radiation emitted when beryllium was bombarded with alpha particles—an experiment that closed the gap in atomic mass that protons alone couldn't explain. Neutrons carry zero charge and a mass nearly identical to protons. Different neutron counts produce isotopes of the same element. On Regents exams, students calculate neutron count using the relationship: neutrons = mass number − atomic number. For example, carbon-14 (mass 14, atomic number 6) contains 8 neutrons.

questions.prompt: Negatively charged particle that occupies regions of space around the nucleus.
questions.explanation: J.J. Thomson discovered the electron in 1897 using cathode-ray tube experiments, showing the particle carried a −1 charge and a mass roughly 1/1836 that of a proton. In the modern wave-mechanical model, electrons occupy orbitals—regions of probability—rather than fixed paths. Their arrangement governs chemical bonding and the periodic trends seen in the NYS Periodic Table. Regents items frequently ask students to identify the particle responsible for chemical behavior or to compare electron arrangements in ground vs. excited states.

questions.prompt: Tiny, dense, positively charged center of an atom containing nearly all of its mass.
questions.explanation: Ernest Rutherford proposed the nuclear model in 1911 after his famous gold-foil experiment, in which alpha particles fired at thin gold foil mostly passed through but a few deflected sharply—evidence of a small, dense, positive core. The nucleus contains protons and neutrons (collectively nucleons) and accounts for over 99.9% of atomic mass while occupying about 1/100,000 of the atom's volume. The NYS Chemistry Reference Tables use this nuclear model when interpreting Table O notation and nuclear decay equations.

questions.prompt: Number equal to the count of protons in the nucleus; printed in each Periodic Table box on the NYS Reference Tables.
questions.explanation: The atomic number (Z) uniquely identifies an element—change Z and you change the element itself. On the NYS Periodic Table, the atomic number is the whole number printed above each element symbol (e.g., Na = 11). Henry Moseley established in 1913 that ordering elements by atomic number, not atomic mass as Mendeleev had done in 1869, resolved inconsistencies in the periodic law. For a neutral atom, atomic number equals both proton count and electron count. Regents items routinely use Z to determine ion charge after electron loss/gain.

questions.prompt: Whole-number sum of protons and neutrons in an atom's nucleus.
questions.explanation: The mass number (A) is the integer sum of protons + neutrons; it appears as a superscript in nuclide notation (e.g., ¹⁴C). Unlike atomic mass, which is a weighted average reported in the NYS Periodic Table box, mass number refers to one specific isotope. To find neutrons, subtract atomic number from mass number: ¹⁴C has 14 − 6 = 8 neutrons. Regents exams use mass number heavily in Table O nuclear decay equations and in identifying isotopes.

questions.prompt: Atoms of the same element having the same number of protons but different numbers of neutrons.
questions.explanation: Isotopes share atomic number but differ in mass number—for example, ¹²C, ¹³C, and ¹⁴C all have 6 protons but 6, 7, and 8 neutrons respectively. Frederick Soddy coined the term 'isotope' in 1913, and the NYS Chemistry Reference Tables Table O lists common radioactive isotopes used in dating and medicine. Because chemical behavior depends almost entirely on electron arrangement, isotopes of the same element show nearly identical chemistry but different nuclear stability and mass.

questions.prompt: Weighted average mass of all naturally occurring isotopes of an element, expressed in atomic mass units.
questions.explanation: Atomic mass is the decimal value printed below each element symbol on the NYS Periodic Table (e.g., Cl = 35.45 amu). It is not the mass of a single atom; rather, it is a weighted average that accounts for the natural abundance of each isotope. Chlorine's value of 35.45 reflects roughly 76% ³⁵Cl and 24% ³⁷Cl. The atomic mass unit is defined as 1/12 the mass of a ¹²C atom. Regents items often supply isotope masses and percent abundances and ask students to calculate the resulting atomic mass.

questions.prompt: Type of average used to calculate atomic mass from isotope masses and their natural abundance percentages.
questions.explanation: A weighted average multiplies each isotope's mass by its decimal abundance and sums the results: atomic mass = Σ(mass × fractional abundance). For chlorine: (34.97 × 0.7577) + (36.97 × 0.2423) ≈ 35.45 amu. Unlike a simple mean, weighted averages skew toward the most abundant isotope—why chlorine's atomic mass sits closer to 35 than to 37. NYS Regents items commonly provide a table of isotope data and require students to perform this calculation and match the result to a Periodic Table entry.

questions.prompt: Symbolic representation of an isotope showing element symbol with mass number on top and atomic number on bottom, like ¹⁴₆C.
questions.explanation: Nuclide notation (also called isotopic notation) writes an isotope with mass number (A) as a superscript and atomic number (Z) as a subscript before the element symbol, such as ²³₁₁Na. This format appears throughout NYS Reference Tables Table O for nuclear decay equations. The notation lets students balance nuclear reactions by ensuring that mass numbers and atomic numbers on each side of the arrow are equal. Regents items use nuclide notation when writing alpha decay (⁴₂He), beta decay (⁰₋₁e), and transmutation equations.

questions.prompt: Notation showing how electrons are distributed across an atom's shells, such as 2-8-1 for sodium.
questions.explanation: Electron configuration lists the number of electrons in each principal energy level from innermost to outermost. The NYS Periodic Table prints this configuration directly in each element box. For sodium (Z=11), the ground-state configuration is 2-8-1, meaning 2 electrons in the first shell, 8 in the second, and 1 valence electron in the third. The Bohr model (1913) introduced this shell-based picture, and Regents items use it to predict ion charges (Na loses 1 electron to form Na⁺) and to compare ground vs. excited states.

questions.prompt: Region of probability around the nucleus where an electron is most likely to be found.
questions.explanation: Orbitals are three-dimensional regions defined by quantum mechanics where electrons reside with high probability. Erwin Schrödinger's 1926 wave equation underpins the orbital concept, replacing the Bohr model's fixed circular paths with probability clouds. The wave-mechanical model recognizes s, p, d, and f orbital shapes. For NYS Chemistry Regents, students need to recognize that the modern view differs from Bohr's planetary picture: electrons don't orbit in fixed paths but occupy orbitals of specific energies.

questions.prompt: Lowest-energy electron arrangement for an atom; configuration printed on the NYS Periodic Table.
questions.explanation: An atom is in the ground state when its electrons occupy the lowest available energy levels, filling inner shells before outer ones. The NYS Periodic Table lists every element's ground-state configuration—lithium reads 2-1, oxygen reads 2-6. When energy is absorbed, an electron can jump to a higher level, putting the atom in an excited state. Regents items distinguish ground from excited states by comparing a given configuration to the Periodic Table value: any deviation that places electrons in higher shells before filling lower ones indicates excitation.

questions.prompt: Higher-energy electron arrangement reached when an atom absorbs energy and an electron jumps to a higher shell.
questions.explanation: In an excited state, one or more electrons occupy a higher principal energy level than the ground-state configuration shown on the NYS Periodic Table. For sodium, ground state is 2-8-1; an excited state might be 2-7-2 (an inner electron promoted) or 2-8-0-1 (the valence electron jumped). When the electron falls back, it releases energy as a photon of specific wavelength, producing the bright-line spectrum (Table—see spectral evidence). Regents items ask students to identify excited configurations and link them to emitted light.

questions.prompt: Electron in the outermost occupied shell; determines bonding behavior and group placement.
questions.explanation: Valence electrons are the outermost-shell electrons—the ones involved in forming chemical bonds. Group number on the NYS Periodic Table predicts valence count: Group 1 elements have 1, Group 2 have 2, Groups 13-18 have 3-8. G.N. Lewis introduced the concept of valence electron sharing in 1916 when he proposed the electron-dot (Lewis) diagram. The octet rule (atoms tend toward 8 valence electrons, like a noble gas) drives most ionic and covalent bonding. Regents items use valence electrons to predict ion charges, draw Lewis structures, and explain periodic reactivity trends.

questions.prompt: Cell on the NYS Periodic Table containing an element's symbol, atomic number, atomic mass, and electron configuration.
questions.explanation: Each Periodic Table box on the NYS Chemistry Reference Tables packs four key pieces of information: chemical symbol (e.g., Fe), atomic number above (26), atomic mass below (55.85), and ground-state electron configuration (2-8-14-2). Some boxes also include common oxidation states. Mendeleev's original 1869 table arranged elements by atomic mass; modern tables follow Moseley's 1913 reordering by atomic number. Regents items routinely require students to read these boxes accurately to answer questions about composition, ions, and properties.

questions.prompt: Visual model showing electrons arranged in concentric rings around the nucleus.
questions.explanation: An electron shell diagram (or Bohr-Rutherford diagram) draws the nucleus at the center with electron shells as rings holding dots representing electrons. Shell 1 holds up to 2 electrons, shell 2 up to 8, shell 3 up to 18 (commonly 8 for Period 3). The diagram makes valence electron count visible at a glance. NYS Chemistry Regents items use these diagrams to ask which element is depicted, to identify ground vs. excited states, or to predict the ion formed (e.g., a Mg diagram with 2-8-2 predicts Mg²⁺ after losing 2 valence electrons).

questions.prompt: Radioactive nuclide listed on Reference Table O along with its decay mode and half-life.
questions.explanation: NYS Reference Tables Table O ('Symbols Used in Nuclear Chemistry') and the companion Table N ('Selected Radioisotopes') list isotopes such as ¹⁴C (half-life 5715 years, beta decay), ²³⁸U (4.47 × 10⁹ years, alpha), and ¹³¹I (8.021 days, beta) along with their decay particles. Regents items reference Table N when calculating remaining mass after a given number of half-lives or when identifying the daughter nuclide produced by a particular decay mode.

questions.prompt: Physical characteristic—like density, melting point, or boiling point—listed on Reference Table S for each element.
questions.explanation: NYS Reference Table S ('Properties of Selected Elements') tabulates first ionization energy, electronegativity, melting point (K), boiling point (K), density (g/cm³ or g/L), and atomic radius (pm) for elements 1-83 (plus 86 and 88). Regents items frequently send students to Table S to compare two elements' melting points, identify the element with the highest ionization energy in a period, or determine which element has the largest atomic radius in a group.

questions.prompt: Specific wavelength of light emitted when an excited electron falls back to a lower energy level.
questions.explanation: When an electron in an excited atom drops to a lower shell, it releases energy as a photon of a specific wavelength, producing a bright-line (emission) spectrum unique to each element. Sodium produces a characteristic yellow doublet near 589 nm, hydrogen produces the Balmer series of visible lines. Niels Bohr explained this in 1913 by linking discrete electron energy levels to discrete photon energies (E = hf). NYS Regents items often show a spectral chart with several elements' lines and ask students to identify an unknown gas in a discharge tube.

questions.prompt: Earliest modern atomic model (1808) describing atoms as indivisible solid spheres.
questions.explanation: John Dalton published his atomic theory in 1808 with five key postulates: matter is composed of atoms; atoms of the same element are identical; atoms cannot be created, divided, or destroyed; atoms combine in fixed whole-number ratios to form compounds; and chemical reactions involve rearrangement of atoms. Dalton's model pictured atoms as tiny, hard, indivisible spheres. While later models added internal structure, Dalton's conservation of atoms still underpins how Regents students balance chemical equations.

questions.prompt: Atomic model proposed in 1904 picturing electrons embedded in a positively charged sphere.
questions.explanation: J.J. Thomson, having discovered the electron in 1897, proposed the 'plum pudding' model in 1904: a positively charged sphere with negative electrons scattered throughout like raisins in a pudding. The model accounted for overall neutrality but placed no concentrated nucleus. Rutherford's 1911 gold-foil experiment soon disproved it by showing that positive charge is concentrated in a tiny nucleus. NYS Regents items use the Thomson model to illustrate how scientific models evolve as new evidence accumulates.

questions.prompt: 1911 atomic model in which a tiny dense positive nucleus is surrounded by mostly empty space.
questions.explanation: Ernest Rutherford's 1911 nuclear model emerged from the gold-foil experiment, in which Hans Geiger and Ernest Marsden fired alpha particles at thin gold foil. Most passed through, but a small fraction deflected sharply, leading Rutherford to conclude that the atom's positive charge and nearly all its mass are concentrated in a tiny central nucleus, surrounded by mostly empty space containing electrons. This replaced Thomson's plum pudding picture and set the stage for Bohr's quantized shells. Regents items use this discovery to explain why most alpha particles passed straight through.

questions.prompt: 1913 atomic model showing electrons in fixed circular orbits at specific energy levels.
questions.explanation: Niels Bohr published his quantized atomic model in 1913, proposing that electrons travel in fixed circular orbits (shells) at specific allowed energies. Electrons absorb energy to jump to higher shells and release photons of specific wavelengths when falling back—explaining the bright-line spectra observed for hydrogen and other elements. The NYS Periodic Table's shell-by-shell electron configurations (e.g., 2-8-1) derive directly from Bohr's model. Although superseded by the wave-mechanical model, the Bohr picture is still how Regents students draw electron-shell diagrams.

questions.prompt: Modern atomic model in which electrons occupy regions of probability rather than fixed orbits.
questions.explanation: The wave-mechanical (quantum-mechanical) model, developed by Erwin Schrödinger (1926) and Werner Heisenberg (1925), replaces Bohr's fixed orbits with orbitals—regions where an electron is most likely to be found. Louis de Broglie's 1924 wave-particle duality showed that electrons behave both as particles and waves, and Heisenberg's uncertainty principle forbids knowing both position and momentum exactly. NYS Chemistry Regents recognizes this as the current model, distinguishing it from the simpler Bohr picture used in shell diagrams.

questions.prompt: Horizontal row on the NYS Periodic Table; elements in the same one have the same number of occupied electron shells.
questions.explanation: A period is a horizontal row on the Periodic Table, numbered 1-7. All elements in a given period share the same highest occupied principal energy level: Period 2 elements (Li-Ne) fill the n=2 shell, Period 3 elements (Na-Ar) fill n=3. Across a period, atomic number, ionization energy, and electronegativity increase while atomic radius decreases—trends visible on NYS Reference Table S. Mendeleev's 1869 periodic table grouped elements roughly by mass; the modern arrangement places elements by atomic number, with periodic properties repeating every row.

questions.prompt: Vertical column on the Periodic Table whose elements share similar chemical properties because they have the same number of valence electrons.
questions.explanation: Groups (numbered 1-18 on the NYS Periodic Table) are vertical columns whose elements have identical valence electron counts and therefore similar chemical behavior. Group 1 (alkali metals) all have 1 valence electron and form +1 ions; Group 17 (halogens) all have 7 valence electrons and form −1 ions; Group 18 (noble gases) have full valence shells and rarely react. Mendeleev's 1869 arrangement first grouped elements by similar properties, even leaving gaps for undiscovered elements (gallium, germanium) that were later found with the predicted properties.

questions.prompt: Another name for a group of elements with similar chemical behavior; examples include alkali metals and halogens.
questions.explanation: Family is a synonym for group on the Periodic Table, emphasizing the shared chemical 'family resemblance.' Named families on the NYS Periodic Table include the alkali metals (Group 1), alkaline earth metals (Group 2), halogens (Group 17), and noble gases (Group 18). Members of a family react similarly because they have identical valence electron configurations—for example, all halogens gain one electron to form a −1 ion. Regents items use family names to predict reactivity, ion charge, and physical state at room temperature.

questions.prompt: Element that is typically shiny, malleable, ductile, and a good conductor of heat and electricity; found on the left and center of the Periodic Table.
questions.explanation: Metals occupy the left and central regions of the NYS Periodic Table (Groups 1-12 plus parts of 13-15), making up over 75% of all elements. Their characteristic properties—luster, malleability, ductility, and high thermal/electrical conductivity—stem from metallic bonding, in which valence electrons are delocalized in a 'sea' that flows freely. Metals tend to lose electrons to form positive ions (cations) and have low electronegativity values on Table S. The metal/nonmetal dividing line steps down from B to Po, with metalloids (semi-metals) along the staircase.

questions.prompt: Element that is typically a poor conductor and tends to gain electrons; found on the upper right of the Periodic Table.
questions.explanation: Nonmetals cluster in the upper-right region of the NYS Periodic Table (plus hydrogen at upper left). They include the halogens, noble gases, oxygen, nitrogen, sulfur, phosphorus, selenium, and carbon. Nonmetals typically have high electronegativity values (see Table S), gain electrons to form negative ions (anions), and form covalent bonds with each other and ionic bonds with metals. At room temperature, nonmetals can be solids (C, S), liquids (Br), or gases (O, N, Cl, noble gases). Regents items use nonmetal identity to predict bond types and ion charges.

questions.prompt: Distance from the nucleus to the outermost electron shell; decreases left to right across a period and increases down a group.
questions.explanation: Atomic radius is reported in picometers (pm) on NYS Reference Table S. Moving left to right across a period, atomic radius decreases because added protons pull the electron cloud closer without adding a new shell (e.g., Na ≈ 190 pm shrinks to Cl ≈ 79 pm in Period 3). Moving down a group, radius increases because each new period adds an outer electron shell (Li ≈ 167 pm grows to Cs ≈ 298 pm in Group 1). Regents items routinely ask students to use Table S to compare two elements' radii or identify the largest atom in a series.

questions.prompt: Size of an ion compared to its neutral atom; cations are smaller and anions are larger.
questions.explanation: When a metal loses electrons to form a cation, the radius shrinks because the same nuclear charge now pulls fewer electrons more tightly (Na 190 pm → Na⁺ 102 pm). When a nonmetal gains electrons to form an anion, the radius grows because added electrons increase electron-electron repulsion while nuclear charge stays the same (Cl 79 pm → Cl⁻ 181 pm). Regents items use ionic radii to compare isoelectronic species or to explain the structure of ionic crystal lattices like NaCl.

questions.prompt: Energy required to remove the most loosely bound electron from a gaseous atom; increases left to right across a period.
questions.explanation: First ionization energy is listed on NYS Reference Table S in kJ/mol. Moving left to right across a period, ionization energy generally increases because nuclear charge grows while shell number stays the same, holding electrons more tightly (Na 496 kJ/mol → Ar 1521 kJ/mol). Moving down a group, ionization energy decreases as outer electrons sit in higher shells, farther from the nucleus and shielded by inner electrons. Noble gases have the highest ionization energies; alkali metals the lowest. Regents items use Table S to identify which element loses an electron most easily.

questions.prompt: Measure of an atom's ability to attract electrons in a chemical bond; fluorine has the highest value on the NYS Periodic Table.
questions.explanation: Electronegativity (Pauling scale) is tabulated on NYS Reference Table S. Fluorine, at 4.0, is the most electronegative element; cesium and francium sit near 0.7-0.8 at the opposite extreme. Across a period, electronegativity increases (Na 0.9 → Cl 3.2); down a group, it decreases. Linus Pauling introduced the scale in 1932. Regents items use electronegativity differences to classify bonds: difference < 0.5 nonpolar covalent, 0.5-1.7 polar covalent, > 1.7 generally ionic. The difference predicts whether electrons are shared equally, shared unequally, or transferred.

questions.prompt: General property of being metallic; increases down a group and decreases left to right across a period.
questions.explanation: Metallic character refers to the tendency to lose electrons, form positive ions, and exhibit metallic properties (luster, conductivity, malleability). It increases down a group as outer electrons become easier to remove (low ionization energy) and decreases left to right across a period as elements become more nonmetallic. Cesium and francium are the most metallic stable elements; fluorine and oxygen the least. Regents items frequently use this trend to compare elements: in Group 1, K is more metallic than Na; in Period 3, Na is more metallic than Mg.

questions.prompt: Group 1 element family characterized by 1 valence electron, soft metals that react violently with water.
questions.explanation: Alkali metals (Li, Na, K, Rb, Cs, Fr) occupy Group 1 on the NYS Periodic Table. Each has 1 valence electron and readily loses it to form a +1 cation, making them the most reactive metals. They are soft enough to cut with a knife, have low melting points (Cs melts at 28°C), and react vigorously with water to produce hydrogen gas and a metal hydroxide (e.g., 2Na + 2H₂O → 2NaOH + H₂). Reactivity increases down the group as ionization energy decreases. Regents items use Group 1 to illustrate periodic reactivity trends and ion formation.

questions.prompt: Group 2 element family with 2 valence electrons; includes magnesium, calcium, and barium.
questions.explanation: Alkaline earth metals (Be, Mg, Ca, Sr, Ba, Ra) occupy Group 2 of the NYS Periodic Table. Each has 2 valence electrons and forms a +2 cation. They are harder, denser, and less reactive than the alkali metals in the same period but still react with water and acids. Magnesium burns brightly in air to form MgO; calcium is essential for bones and shells. Reactivity increases down the group. Regents items often pair Group 2 with Group 1 to compare ionization energies, ion charges, and reaction products.

questions.prompt: Group 17 element family with 7 valence electrons; the most reactive nonmetals, forming −1 ions.
questions.explanation: Halogens (F, Cl, Br, I, At) occupy Group 17 on the NYS Periodic Table. With 7 valence electrons, each gains 1 electron to achieve a stable octet, forming a −1 anion. Reactivity decreases down the group because larger atoms attract incoming electrons less strongly. At room temperature, fluorine and chlorine are gases, bromine is a liquid, iodine is a solid. Halogens form diatomic molecules (F₂, Cl₂) and react with metals to produce salts—the word 'halogen' means 'salt-former.' Regents items use halogens for ionic bonding examples and single-replacement reactions.

questions.prompt: Group 18 element family with full valence shells; nearly all are inert and exist as monatomic gases.
questions.explanation: Noble gases (He, Ne, Ar, Kr, Xe, Rn) occupy Group 18 of the NYS Periodic Table. They have complete valence shells (2 electrons for He, 8 for the rest) and therefore extremely high ionization energies—virtually no tendency to gain, lose, or share electrons. Sir William Ramsay isolated most noble gases between 1894 and 1898. Argon makes up about 1% of Earth's atmosphere. Regents items use noble gas configurations (He, Ne, Ar, Kr, Xe, Rn) as the target stable electron arrangement that other atoms achieve through bonding (the octet rule).

questions.prompt: Element in Groups 3-12 whose ions often have variable charges and form colorful compounds.
questions.explanation: Transition elements (Groups 3-12 on the NYS Periodic Table) include iron, copper, zinc, silver, and gold. Their distinguishing feature is partially filled d-orbitals, which allow multiple oxidation states (Fe²⁺ and Fe³⁺; Cu⁺ and Cu²⁺) and produce characteristically colored compounds (CuSO₄ blue, KMnO₄ purple). Transition metals are denser, harder, and have higher melting points than alkali or alkaline earth metals. NYS Periodic Table boxes list multiple oxidation states for many transition elements. Regents items use them in redox problems and to illustrate variable ion charge.

questions.prompt: Tendency for atoms to gain, lose, or share electrons until they have 8 valence electrons like a noble gas.
questions.explanation: The octet rule, articulated by Gilbert N. Lewis in 1916, states that atoms tend to form bonds so that each has 8 valence electrons—matching the stable configuration of noble gases (except He, which is stable with 2). Sodium loses 1 electron to mimic neon's 2-8 configuration; chlorine gains 1 electron to mimic argon's 2-8-8. Hydrogen seeks 2 (helium-like). Exceptions include H, Li, Be, and some d-block elements. NYS Chemistry Regents uses the octet rule to predict ion formation, draw Lewis structures, and explain why ionic and covalent bonds form.

questions.prompt: Net positive or negative number an atom carries after losing or gaining electrons to achieve a stable configuration.
questions.explanation: Ion charge equals (protons − electrons). Metals lose electrons to form positive charges equal to their group number (Group 1: +1, Group 2: +2, Group 13: +3); nonmetals gain electrons to form negative charges equal to (group number − 18) (Group 17: −1, Group 16: −2). The NYS Periodic Table lists common oxidation states in each box. For transition metals, multiple ion charges are possible (Fe²⁺ vs. Fe³⁺). Regents items use ion charges to write correct chemical formulas (e.g., Al³⁺ + O²⁻ → Al₂O₃) and to predict products of reactions.

questions.prompt: Positively charged ion formed when an atom loses one or more electrons.
questions.explanation: A cation is a positive ion produced when an atom loses electrons. Metals (especially Groups 1, 2, and 13) readily form cations because they have low ionization energies. Sodium loses 1 electron to become Na⁺; magnesium loses 2 to become Mg²⁺; aluminum loses 3 to become Al³⁺. Transition metals can form multiple cations (Cu⁺, Cu²⁺, Fe²⁺, Fe³⁺). NYS Regents items use cation formation to explain ionic bonding, electroplating (Table J), and electrochemistry. The term comes from the Greek 'kation' meaning 'going down' (toward the cathode).

questions.prompt: Negatively charged ion formed when an atom gains one or more electrons.
questions.explanation: An anion is a negative ion produced when an atom gains electrons. Nonmetals (especially halogens, Group 16, and Group 15) readily form anions because of their high electronegativity. Chlorine gains 1 electron to become Cl⁻; oxygen gains 2 to form O²⁻; nitrogen gains 3 to form N³⁻. Polyatomic anions (NO₃⁻, SO₄²⁻, CO₃²⁻) appear on NYS Reference Table E. Anions migrate toward the anode in electrolytic cells. The term comes from the Greek 'anion' meaning 'going up.' Regents items use anion formation in ionic compound naming and in salt formula prediction.

questions.prompt: Electron arrangement matching that of a noble gas; the target configuration for most ions.
questions.explanation: A stable electron configuration matches that of a noble gas—typically 8 valence electrons (octet) or 2 for the smallest atoms (duet, He-like). Atoms achieve stability by gaining, losing, or sharing electrons. Na⁺ has the configuration of Ne (2-8); Cl⁻ has the configuration of Ar (2-8-8); Mg²⁺ has the configuration of Ne. This drives ionic bond formation: the lower-electronegativity atom donates electrons; the higher-electronegativity atom accepts them. NYS Chemistry Regents asks students to identify which noble gas an ion is isoelectronic with by comparing electron counts.

questions.prompt: Pattern showing how chemical reactivity changes within a group; metals increase down a group, nonmetals decrease.
questions.explanation: The reactivity trend on the NYS Periodic Table follows ionization energy and electronegativity. For metals, reactivity increases down a group (Cs > Rb > K > Na > Li) because larger atoms lose electrons more easily. For nonmetals, reactivity decreases down a group (F > Cl > Br > I) because smaller atoms attract incoming electrons more strongly. Across a period, metallic reactivity decreases left to right; nonmetallic reactivity increases. NYS Reference Table J ('Activity Series') ranks metals by reactivity, predicting which can replace another in a single-replacement reaction.

questions.prompt: Law stating that properties of the elements are periodic functions of their atomic numbers.
questions.explanation: The modern periodic law states that physical and chemical properties of elements recur in regular patterns when elements are arranged by increasing atomic number. Dmitri Mendeleev first formulated a periodic law in 1869 based on atomic mass, but Henry Moseley reordered the table by atomic number in 1913 after measuring X-ray frequencies, resolving anomalies like Co/Ni and Te/I. The law underpins every trend on NYS Reference Table S—atomic radius, ionization energy, electronegativity—and explains why elements in the same group share chemical behavior.

questions.prompt: Pattern observed in Group 1: increasing reactivity, decreasing ionization energy, and increasing atomic radius down the group.
questions.explanation: Group 1 (alkali metals) display the clearest periodic trends on the NYS Periodic Table. From Li to Cs, atomic radius increases (167 pm → 298 pm), ionization energy decreases (520 → 376 kJ/mol per Table S), electronegativity decreases (1.0 → 0.8), and reactivity with water increases dramatically. Lithium reacts mildly; sodium fizzes; potassium burns; rubidium and cesium can explode. All form +1 ions and white ionic compounds with halogens. Regents items use Group 1 as the textbook example of periodicity within a group.

questions.prompt: Pattern observed in Group 17: decreasing reactivity, increasing atomic radius, and varying physical states from gas to solid down the group.
questions.explanation: Group 17 (halogens) on the NYS Periodic Table show coordinated trends: from F to I, atomic radius increases (71 pm → 133 pm), electronegativity decreases (4.0 → 2.7), and reactivity decreases. Physical state changes top to bottom: F₂ and Cl₂ are gases, Br₂ is a reddish liquid, I₂ is a purple solid—reflecting increasing London dispersion forces with larger electron clouds. All halogens form diatomic molecules and gain one electron to form −1 ions. Regents items use Group 17 trends in single-replacement reactions: Cl₂ can displace Br⁻ or I⁻ but not F⁻.

questions.prompt: Trend on Reference Table S that students use to predict relative atomic radius, electronegativity, or ionization energy.
questions.explanation: NYS Reference Table S ('Properties of Selected Elements') tabulates atomic radius (pm), first ionization energy (kJ/mol), electronegativity (Pauling), melting point (K), boiling point (K), and density. Students use Table S to verify periodic trends quantitatively: in Period 3, Na (190 pm) > Mg (160 pm) > Al (124 pm) > Si (114 pm) > P (109 pm) > S (104 pm) > Cl (79 pm). NYS Regents items frequently require Table S lookups to compare two elements, identify the largest atom in a list, or determine which element has the highest first ionization energy.

questions.prompt: Bond formed when one atom transfers electrons to another, producing oppositely charged ions held together by electrostatic attraction.
questions.explanation: An ionic bond forms when a metal transfers one or more electrons to a nonmetal, creating a cation and an anion that attract each other electrostatically. Sodium loses 1 electron to chlorine to form NaCl, with a crystal lattice of alternating Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions. NYS Reference Table S electronegativity differences greater than ~1.7 typically indicate ionic bonding. Ionic compounds have high melting points (NaCl melts at 801°C), are brittle, dissolve in water to conduct electricity, and form crystalline solids. Regents items ask students to predict ionic formulas from group numbers.

questions.prompt: Bond formed when two atoms share electrons rather than transferring them.
questions.explanation: A covalent bond is formed when two nonmetal atoms share one or more pairs of electrons. Hydrogen forms H₂ by sharing one electron pair; oxygen forms O₂ with a double bond. Gilbert N. Lewis introduced shared-pair bonding in 1916. Electronegativity differences below ~1.7 generally indicate covalent bonds; differences below 0.5 are nonpolar covalent. Covalent compounds typically have lower melting points than ionic compounds, are poor conductors, and may be molecular gases, liquids, or soft solids. NYS Regents items use Lewis structures to identify covalent bonds and predict molecular shapes.

questions.prompt: Bond in metals described as a 'sea of electrons' shared among all atoms, explaining conductivity and malleability.
questions.explanation: Metallic bonding pictures positive metal ions arranged in a lattice, surrounded by a delocalized 'sea' of valence electrons free to move throughout the structure. This explains the characteristic metallic properties: high electrical and thermal conductivity (electrons flow freely), malleability and ductility (atoms can slide past each other without breaking the bond), and metallic luster (free electrons absorb and re-emit light). The NYS Periodic Table groups bonded by metallic character on the left and center. Regents items use metallic bonding to explain copper wire conductivity and gold's malleability.

questions.prompt: Covalent bond in which electrons are shared unequally because of an electronegativity difference between the atoms.
questions.explanation: A polar covalent bond forms between atoms with an electronegativity difference between roughly 0.5 and 1.7 (using NYS Table S values). The more electronegative atom holds the shared electrons more tightly, creating partial charges (δ⁻ on one atom, δ⁺ on the other). Water's O-H bonds (ΔEN ≈ 1.4) are classic polar covalent bonds, giving water its dipole moment and many of its unique properties. Regents items test students' ability to use Table S to calculate ΔEN and classify a bond as nonpolar covalent, polar covalent, or ionic.

questions.prompt: Covalent bond in which electrons are shared equally because the bonded atoms have identical or very similar electronegativities.
questions.explanation: A nonpolar covalent bond forms when two atoms share electrons equally—their electronegativity difference is below about 0.5 on NYS Table S. Diatomic molecules of the same element (H₂, O₂, N₂, F₂, Cl₂) have zero difference and are perfectly nonpolar. C-H bonds (ΔEN ≈ 0.4) are usually treated as essentially nonpolar in NYS Chemistry. Nonpolar covalent compounds are generally insoluble in water but soluble in nonpolar solvents (the 'like dissolves like' rule). Regents items use ΔEN values to distinguish nonpolar from polar covalent.

questions.prompt: Diagram using dots around an element symbol to represent valence electrons.
questions.explanation: Lewis dot diagrams (or electron-dot structures) represent valence electrons as dots around an element symbol, introduced by Gilbert N. Lewis in 1916. For sodium (1 valence electron): Na•. For chlorine (7 valence electrons): :Cl: with 3 lone pairs and 1 single electron. The diagram makes valence count, lone pairs, and bonding pairs visible. Lewis structures are essential for predicting molecular geometry via VSEPR theory. NYS Chemistry Regents items ask students to draw Lewis dot diagrams of atoms and simple molecules (H₂O, NH₃, CO₂) and to identify bonding vs. lone-pair electrons.

questions.prompt: Two electrons shared between atoms forming a single covalent bond, shown as a dash or two dots between symbols.
questions.explanation: A shared pair (bonding pair) consists of two electrons—one typically contributed by each atom—occupying the space between two nuclei to form a covalent bond. In a Lewis structure, a shared pair is drawn as two dots or as a single dash (H-H for H₂). Each shared pair counts as one covalent bond. Hydrogen needs one shared pair to satisfy its duet; carbon typically needs four to satisfy its octet. NYS Regents items use shared pairs in Lewis structures to count bonds and to distinguish single, double, and triple bonds.

questions.prompt: Valence electron pair on a single atom that is not involved in bonding.
questions.explanation: A lone pair (nonbonding pair) is a pair of valence electrons not shared between atoms—it belongs entirely to one atom. In water (H₂O), the oxygen has 2 lone pairs in addition to 2 bonding pairs with the hydrogens, giving the molecule its characteristic bent shape. Lone pairs occupy more space than bonding pairs and influence molecular geometry through VSEPR theory: they push bonding pairs closer together. NYS Regents items use lone pairs to explain water's bent shape and ammonia's pyramidal geometry, and to predict polarity.

questions.prompt: Covalent bond formed when two pairs of electrons are shared between the same two atoms, shown as = between symbols.
questions.explanation: A double bond involves two shared electron pairs (4 total electrons) between the same two atoms—shown as a double dash (=) in structural formulas. Oxygen gas (O=O), carbon dioxide (O=C=O), and ethene (H₂C=CH₂) all contain double bonds. Double bonds are shorter and stronger than single bonds but allow no rotation. The pi-bond component prevents free rotation, locking molecular geometry in place. NYS Regents items use double bonds to distinguish alkenes from alkanes in organic chemistry and to draw correct Lewis structures for O₂ and CO₂.

questions.prompt: Alternative Lewis representation needed when a single structure cannot fully describe the bonding (like in ozone or the carbonate ion).
questions.explanation: Resonance structures are two or more valid Lewis diagrams that differ only in the placement of double bonds and lone pairs—the real molecule is a hybrid average of all contributing structures. Linus Pauling formalized resonance theory in the 1930s. Common Regents examples include ozone (O₃), where the central oxygen forms a double bond with one terminal O and a single bond with the other, and the actual bond lengths are equal—intermediate between single and double. Other examples: carbonate (CO₃²⁻), nitrate (NO₃⁻), and benzene (C₆H₆).

questions.prompt: Theory predicting molecular geometry based on the idea that electron pairs around a central atom repel each other and arrange to be as far apart as possible.
questions.explanation: Valence Shell Electron Pair Repulsion (VSEPR) theory, developed by Ronald Gillespie and Ronald Nyholm in 1957, predicts molecular shape from the number of bonding pairs and lone pairs around a central atom. Common geometries: 2 pairs → linear (CO₂), 3 → trigonal planar (BF₃), 4 → tetrahedral (CH₄), 4 with one lone pair → pyramidal (NH₃), 4 with two lone pairs → bent (H₂O). VSEPR explains why water is bent rather than linear: oxygen's two lone pairs push the H atoms closer together (~104.5° bond angle). NYS Regents items use VSEPR to predict polarity.

questions.prompt: Molecular shape in which atoms are arranged in a straight line with a 180° bond angle.
questions.explanation: A linear molecule has atoms arranged in a straight line with a 180° bond angle, occurring when the central atom has 2 bonding pairs and no lone pairs (or in some cases other arrangements that produce linearity). Examples include CO₂ (O=C=O), HCN, and BeCl₂. All diatomic molecules (H₂, O₂, N₂, HCl) are linear by definition. Linear molecules can be nonpolar even if their bonds are polar—CO₂'s two C=O dipoles cancel because they point in opposite directions. NYS Regents items use linear geometry to explain CO₂'s nonpolarity despite polar bonds.

questions.prompt: Molecular shape with one lone pair on the central atom, producing an angled geometry like that of water.
questions.explanation: A bent (or angular) molecule has a central atom with 2 bonding pairs and 1 or 2 lone pairs. Water (H₂O) is the textbook bent molecule: oxygen has 2 bonding pairs (to H) and 2 lone pairs, giving an H-O-H angle of about 104.5° rather than the 109.5° of a perfect tetrahedron because lone pairs repel more strongly than bonding pairs. SO₂ is also bent. Bent geometry combined with polar bonds makes water highly polar—a key fact for explaining its high boiling point, surface tension, and ability to dissolve ionic compounds. Regents items use bent shape to distinguish polar from nonpolar molecules.

questions.prompt: Molecular geometry with three atoms arranged at 120° angles around a central atom in a flat plane.
questions.explanation: A trigonal planar molecule has a central atom bonded to three others in a flat, triangular arrangement with bond angles of 120°. The central atom has 3 bonding pairs and no lone pairs. Examples include BF₃, BCl₃, SO₃, and the carbonate ion (CO₃²⁻). Like CO₂'s linear geometry, trigonal planar molecules with identical bonded atoms are nonpolar because their bond dipoles cancel by symmetry. NYS Regents items use trigonal planar geometry in resonance structures (NO₃⁻, CO₃²⁻) and in explaining the bonding around the carbon in carbonyl groups.

questions.prompt: 3D molecular geometry with four atoms at the corners of a regular tetrahedron, 109.5° bond angles.
questions.explanation: A tetrahedral molecule has a central atom bonded to four others at the corners of a tetrahedron with bond angles of 109.5°. The central atom has 4 bonding pairs and no lone pairs. Methane (CH₄), CCl₄, and SiF₄ are textbook examples. The tetrahedral arrangement minimizes electron-pair repulsion in 3D space. When four identical atoms bond to a tetrahedral center (CH₄), the molecule is nonpolar despite polar bonds because dipoles cancel by symmetry. NYS Regents items use tetrahedral geometry to introduce molecular shape and to explain why CH₄ is nonpolar.

questions.prompt: Strong attractive force between molecules in which hydrogen is bonded directly to N, O, or F.
questions.explanation: Hydrogen bonding is the strongest type of intermolecular force, occurring when a hydrogen atom bonded to a small, highly electronegative atom (N, O, or F) is attracted to a lone pair on another N, O, or F. The H-N, H-O, or H-F bond creates an exceptionally large partial positive on H. Hydrogen bonds explain water's unusually high boiling point (100°C, far higher than H₂S at -60°C), DNA base pairing, and ice's lower density than liquid water. NYS Regents items use hydrogen bonding to explain water's anomalous properties and to predict boiling-point trends.

questions.prompt: Attraction between the partial positive end of one polar molecule and the partial negative end of another.
questions.explanation: Dipole-dipole forces are intermolecular attractions between polar molecules, in which the δ⁺ end of one molecule attracts the δ⁻ end of another. They are weaker than hydrogen bonds and ionic bonds but stronger than London dispersion forces. HCl (μ ≈ 1.08 D), SO₂, and CH₃Cl are polar molecules held by dipole-dipole forces. The strength depends on molecular polarity, which can be predicted from electronegativity differences and molecular geometry. NYS Regents items use dipole-dipole forces to explain why polar covalent compounds have higher boiling points than similarly sized nonpolar compounds.

questions.prompt: Weakest intermolecular force, present in all molecules, caused by temporary instantaneous dipoles between electron clouds.
questions.explanation: London dispersion forces (named for Fritz London, 1930) arise from temporary fluctuations in electron distribution that create instantaneous dipoles. They exist between all molecules but are the only intermolecular force in nonpolar substances like methane, noble gases, and hydrocarbons. Their strength increases with molecular size: butane (C₄H₁₀) boils at -1°C; octane (C₈H₁₈) boils at 126°C—solely because larger molecules have more polarizable electron clouds. NYS Regents items use London forces to explain trends in boiling points of nonpolar molecules and the diatomic halogens' state changes (F₂, Cl₂ gas → Br₂ liquid → I₂ solid).

questions.prompt: Property of a molecule with an uneven distribution of charge resulting from polar bonds and an asymmetric geometry.
questions.explanation: Molecular polarity refers to whether a molecule has a net dipole moment. A polar molecule has polar bonds AND an asymmetric geometry that prevents bond dipoles from canceling (water is bent, so its O-H dipoles add). A nonpolar molecule either lacks polar bonds (O₂, CH₄) or has symmetric geometry that cancels them (CO₂ is linear; CCl₄ is tetrahedral). Polarity determines solubility ('like dissolves like'), boiling point, and intermolecular forces. NYS Regents items use Lewis structures plus VSEPR to predict polarity and compare it across molecules.

questions.prompt: Property of a liquid's surface that resists penetration; caused by cohesive intermolecular forces.
questions.explanation: Surface tension is the cohesive force at the surface of a liquid that resists external forces. Molecules at the surface experience attraction only from below and from the sides, not above, creating a 'skin' effect. Water has unusually high surface tension (72 mN/m) due to extensive hydrogen bonding—why insects can walk on water and why water beads up on waxed surfaces. Mercury's surface tension is even higher (485 mN/m) thanks to strong metallic bonding. Surface tension decreases with temperature as molecules gain kinetic energy. NYS Regents items use surface tension to illustrate the effects of intermolecular forces.

questions.prompt: Temperature at which a solid changes to a liquid; ionic compounds have very high values while molecular solids generally have low ones.
questions.explanation: Melting point is the temperature at which a solid transitions to a liquid—energy must overcome the attractive forces holding particles in the crystal lattice. Ionic compounds (NaCl, 801°C; MgO, 2852°C) melt at very high temperatures because strong electrostatic forces must be disrupted. Network covalent solids like diamond (3550°C) and quartz (1610°C) are even higher. Molecular solids (ice, 0°C; sugar, 186°C) melt at lower temperatures because only intermolecular forces must be broken. NYS Reference Table S lists melting points in Kelvin for selected elements. Regents items compare melting points to deduce bond type.

questions.prompt: Ability of a substance to conduct an electric current; depends on the presence of mobile charged particles.
questions.explanation: Electrical conductivity requires mobile charged particles—either free electrons (metals) or free ions (electrolytes in solution or molten state). Solid ionic compounds do not conduct because ions are locked in the crystal lattice, but they conduct when dissolved or melted (NaCl(aq), NaCl(l)). Molecular compounds generally do not conduct because they contain no ions or free electrons. Metals conduct in any state due to the delocalized electron sea. NYS Regents items use conductivity tests to distinguish electrolytes from nonelectrolytes and to identify metallic bonding.

questions.prompt: Regular, repeating 3D arrangement of particles in an ionic or metallic solid.
questions.explanation: A crystal lattice is the ordered, repeating three-dimensional arrangement of particles in a crystalline solid. In ionic compounds like NaCl, the lattice consists of alternating Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions arranged in a cubic pattern, with each ion surrounded by six oppositely charged neighbors. In metals, positive metal ions sit in a lattice surrounded by a sea of delocalized electrons. The strong attractions throughout the lattice give ionic and metallic solids their high melting points, hardness, and characteristic shapes (NaCl forms cubic crystals). NYS Regents items use crystal lattice structure to explain ionic-compound properties.

questions.prompt: Property of metals describing how they can be hammered or rolled into thin sheets without breaking.
questions.explanation: Malleability is the ability of a solid to be deformed under compressive stress, like hammered into thin sheets. Metals are highly malleable because their metallic bonding allows atoms (or rather, positive ions) to slide past each other while the delocalized electron sea readjusts, maintaining the bond throughout the deformation. Gold can be hammered into leaf only 100 nm thick. Ionic crystals are brittle, not malleable: when stress shifts ions, like charges align and repel, shattering the crystal. NYS Regents items use malleability to distinguish metallic from ionic bonding and to explain how metals can be shaped.

questions.prompt: Rule for predicting solubility: polar solvents dissolve polar/ionic solutes, while nonpolar solvents dissolve nonpolar solutes.
questions.explanation: Solubility patterns follow the rule 'like dissolves like': polar solvents (water) dissolve polar molecules and ionic compounds; nonpolar solvents (hexane, oil) dissolve nonpolar molecules. Water's polarity and hydrogen bonding allow it to surround and separate Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions, dissolving NaCl. Oil and water don't mix because water can't form favorable interactions with nonpolar oil molecules. NYS Reference Table F ('Solubility Guidelines') lists which ionic compounds are soluble in water. Regents items use polarity and Table F to predict whether a substance dissolves and to identify precipitation reactions.

questions.prompt: Phase of matter with definite shape and definite volume; particles vibrate in fixed positions.
questions.explanation: Solids have definite shape and volume because their particles are held in fixed positions by strong intermolecular or ionic forces, vibrating in place rather than translating. Crystalline solids (NaCl, ice) have a regular repeating lattice; amorphous solids (glass) lack long-range order. The NYS Reference Table H ('Vapor Pressure of Four Liquids') and Table S melting/boiling points let students predict when a substance is solid at a given temperature. Regents items use solid-state properties to identify ionic, molecular, network, or metallic bonding from the substance's behavior.

questions.prompt: Transformation from one physical state to another, like melting or boiling; involves a change in arrangement of particles but not composition.
questions.explanation: Phase change is a physical transformation between solid, liquid, and gas states. The six named phase changes are: melting (solid→liquid), freezing (liquid→solid), vaporization (liquid→gas), condensation (gas→liquid), sublimation (solid→gas, e.g., dry ice), and deposition (gas→solid, e.g., frost). All are physical changes—composition doesn't change. They require heat input (endothermic: melt, vaporize, sublime) or release heat (exothermic: freeze, condense, deposit). On a heating curve, phase changes appear as horizontal plateaus where temperature stays constant while energy breaks intermolecular forces.

questions.prompt: Theory describing gases as a collection of small particles in constant random motion, colliding elastically.
questions.explanation: Kinetic molecular theory (KMT), developed by Boltzmann, Maxwell, and Clausius in the 1800s, models ideal gases with five postulates: (1) gas particles are tiny and far apart (negligible volume); (2) they move in constant random straight-line motion; (3) collisions are perfectly elastic (no kinetic energy lost); (4) no attractive or repulsive forces exist between particles; (5) average kinetic energy is directly proportional to absolute temperature in Kelvin. KMT explains pressure (particle collisions with container walls), Boyle's law, Charles's law, and the ideal gas equation used throughout NYS Regents.

questions.prompt: Measure of average kinetic energy of particles in a sample; measured in Kelvin on the absolute scale.
questions.explanation: Temperature is a measure of the average kinetic energy of the particles in a substance. Higher temperature means faster average particle motion. The Celsius scale (°C) sets 0 at water's freezing point and 100 at boiling at 1 atm. The Kelvin scale, established by Lord Kelvin in 1848, starts at absolute zero (−273.15°C = 0 K) where particle motion essentially stops. Gas laws (Charles's, combined, ideal) require Kelvin. NYS Reference Table T provides T(K) = T(°C) + 273. Regents items frequently require students to convert Celsius to Kelvin before applying gas laws.

questions.prompt: Quantity proportional to absolute temperature; all particles in a sample at the same temperature have this same value on average.
questions.explanation: Average kinetic energy (KE_avg = ½ mv²_avg) of particles is directly proportional to absolute temperature in Kelvin: KE_avg = (3/2)kT, where k is Boltzmann's constant. Two gases at the same temperature have the same average KE, but the lighter gas moves faster (Graham's law). When temperature doubles in Kelvin, average KE doubles. NYS Regents items use this relationship to explain why reaction rate increases with temperature (more particles have energy ≥ activation energy) and why gas pressure increases with temperature at constant volume.

questions.prompt: Temperature at which particle motion is at its theoretical minimum; equals 0 Kelvin or −273°C.
questions.explanation: Absolute zero (0 K = −273.15°C) is the theoretical lower limit of temperature, where particles have minimum possible kinetic energy. The third law of thermodynamics states that absolute zero cannot be reached, only approached. Lord Kelvin proposed this in 1848 after observing that gas volumes extrapolated linearly toward zero at this temperature (Charles's law). The Kelvin scale starts here, eliminating negative values in gas-law calculations. Modern experiments have reached temperatures below 1 nK using laser cooling. NYS Regents items use absolute zero to anchor the Kelvin scale conversion and to explain why Kelvin must be used in gas-law problems.

questions.prompt: Temperature scale with absolute zero at zero, used in all gas-law calculations on the NYS Reference Tables.
questions.explanation: The Kelvin scale, established by Lord Kelvin (William Thomson) in 1848, places absolute zero at 0 K. To convert from Celsius: K = °C + 273 (NYS Reference Table T). Kelvin temperatures are always positive and proportional to average kinetic energy, making the scale essential for gas-law calculations. Doubling Kelvin doubles average KE; halving it halves average KE. Charles's law (V/T = constant), the combined gas law, and the ideal gas equation all require Kelvin. NYS Regents items penalize students who plug Celsius into gas-law formulas, so the conversion is one of the most important habits to develop.

questions.prompt: Gas law stating that at constant temperature, the pressure of a gas is inversely proportional to its volume: P₁V₁ = P₂V₂.
questions.explanation: Boyle's law (Robert Boyle, 1662) states that at constant temperature and amount of gas, pressure and volume are inversely proportional: PV = constant, or P₁V₁ = P₂V₂. Squeeze a gas into half its volume, and its pressure doubles. This holds because compression makes particles hit the container walls more often per second. Plotting P vs. V gives a hyperbola; P vs. 1/V gives a straight line. The combined gas law on NYS Reference Table T (P₁V₁/T₁ = P₂V₂/T₂) reduces to Boyle's law when T is constant. Regents items frequently test Boyle's law with simple inverse-proportion problems.

questions.prompt: Gas law stating that at constant pressure, the volume of a gas is directly proportional to its absolute temperature: V₁/T₁ = V₂/T₂.
questions.explanation: Charles's law (Jacques Charles, c. 1787) states that at constant pressure and amount of gas, volume and absolute temperature are directly proportional: V/T = constant, or V₁/T₁ = V₂/T₂. Double the Kelvin temperature, double the volume. Extrapolating the V-vs-T line for any gas points to V = 0 at T = 0 K—evidence for absolute zero. The law assumes temperature in Kelvin. Charles's law underlies hot-air balloons: heating air at constant atmospheric pressure expands it, decreasing density. NYS Reference Table T's combined gas law simplifies to Charles's law when P is constant. Regents items test the V-T relationship.

questions.prompt: Single law combining Boyle's and Charles's relationships: P₁V₁/T₁ = P₂V₂/T₂; printed on NYS Reference Table T.
questions.explanation: The combined gas law unifies Boyle's law and Charles's law: P₁V₁/T₁ = P₂V₂/T₂, where T is in Kelvin. It applies whenever the amount of gas (moles) stays constant. This is one of the most-used equations in Regents Chemistry—printed on NYS Reference Table T—because real problems usually involve all three variables changing. To solve, students set up the equation, plug in given values (always converting to Kelvin), solve for the unknown. NYS Regents items commonly provide initial P, V, and T plus two of the three final values and ask students to find the missing variable.

questions.prompt: Equation of state for an ideal gas: PV = nRT, relating pressure, volume, moles, and temperature.
questions.explanation: The ideal gas law combines all gas laws into PV = nRT, where P is pressure, V is volume, n is moles, R is the universal gas constant, and T is Kelvin. With R = 8.31 L·kPa/(mol·K) or 0.0821 L·atm/(mol·K), the equation works for ideal gases—a model that assumes no intermolecular forces and negligible particle volume. Real gases approach ideal behavior at low pressures and high temperatures. The equation predicts that 1 mole of any gas at STP (273 K, 101.3 kPa) occupies 22.4 L. Although NYS Chemistry Regents focuses more on combined gas law than PV = nRT, the ideal gas model underlies all gas-law reasoning.

questions.prompt: Principle stating that equal volumes of all gases at the same temperature and pressure contain the same number of molecules.
questions.explanation: Avogadro's law (Amedeo Avogadro, 1811) states that equal volumes of gases at the same temperature and pressure contain equal numbers of particles, regardless of identity. From this comes the molar volume: 1 mole of any gas at STP (273 K, 101.3 kPa) occupies 22.4 L and contains 6.022 × 10²³ particles (Avogadro's number). The number 6.022 × 10²³ wasn't determined until the early 20th century (Perrin, Millikan), but the underlying principle was Avogadro's. NYS Reference Tables list Avogadro's number, and Regents items use it for mole-particle conversions and for connecting gas volumes to particle counts.

questions.prompt: Energy required to melt one gram of a solid at its melting point without changing temperature; for water, it's 334 J/g.
questions.explanation: Heat of fusion (Hf) is the energy required to convert 1 g of solid to liquid at its melting point. For water, Hf = 334 J/g (NYS Reference Table B). During melting, this energy breaks the intermolecular forces holding the solid lattice together while temperature stays constant—why heating curves have a horizontal plateau at the melting point. To calculate total heat: q = mHf, where m is mass. For example, melting 50 g of ice at 0°C requires 50 × 334 = 16,700 J. The reverse process, freezing, releases the same energy. NYS Regents items use Table B values to calculate energy in phase-change problems.

questions.prompt: Energy required to vaporize one gram of a liquid at its boiling point; for water, it's 2260 J/g.
questions.explanation: Heat of vaporization (Hv) is the energy required to convert 1 g of liquid to gas at its boiling point. For water, Hv = 2260 J/g (NYS Reference Table B)—much higher than heat of fusion because all intermolecular forces (including extensive hydrogen bonding) must be completely overcome. To boil 100 g of water at 100°C requires 100 × 2260 = 226,000 J. The high Hv of water means sweating cools the body very effectively. The reverse process, condensation, releases the same energy—why steam burns are so dangerous. NYS Regents items use Table B values to calculate heat for vaporization problems.

questions.prompt: Energy needed to raise the temperature of 1 gram of a substance by 1°C; for liquid water, it's 4.18 J/(g·°C).
questions.explanation: Specific heat capacity (C) is the energy required to raise the temperature of 1 g of a substance by 1°C (or 1 K). NYS Reference Table B lists C for water in three phases: ice 2.10 J/(g·°C), liquid 4.18 J/(g·°C), steam 2.01 J/(g·°C). Water's liquid value is unusually high—why oceans moderate climate. The heat equation is q = mCΔT, where m is mass and ΔT is temperature change. For example, heating 100 g of water from 20°C to 50°C requires 100 × 4.18 × 30 = 12,540 J. NYS Regents items use this equation throughout calorimetry and energy problems.

questions.prompt: Graph showing how temperature changes as heat is added to a substance through solid, liquid, and gas phases; has plateaus at melting and boiling points.
questions.explanation: A heating curve plots temperature (y-axis) versus heat added (x-axis) and shows five segments: (1) warming solid (slope = 1/(mC_solid)), (2) melting plateau at the melting point, (3) warming liquid (slope = 1/(mC_liquid)), (4) vaporization plateau at the boiling point, (5) warming gas. During plateaus, temperature stays constant because added energy breaks intermolecular forces rather than increasing kinetic energy. For water, the curve plateaus at 0°C and 100°C. NYS Regents items use heating curves to test all of: phase identification, energy calculation with q = mCΔT and q = mHf or mHv, and reading specific values from graphs.

questions.prompt: Horizontal segment on a heating curve where temperature stays constant while a phase change occurs and potential energy increases.
questions.explanation: On a heating curve, plateaus appear at the melting and boiling points—horizontal segments where added heat goes entirely into breaking intermolecular forces (increasing potential energy) rather than into kinetic energy (which would raise temperature). At the melting plateau, the substance is part solid, part liquid; at the vaporization plateau, part liquid, part gas. The length of each plateau is proportional to the heat needed for that phase change (q = mHf for melting; q = mHv for vaporization). NYS Regents items use plateau analysis to distinguish potential energy from kinetic energy changes and to calculate phase-change heats.

questions.prompt: Matter with a fixed composition and definite properties; either an element or a compound.
questions.explanation: A pure substance has a fixed composition and uniform properties throughout. Elements (cannot be broken down chemically; listed on the Periodic Table) and compounds (chemically bonded combinations of elements in fixed ratios, like H₂O or NaCl) are both pure substances. Pure substances have sharp melting and boiling points and can be represented by a single chemical formula. Compare to mixtures, which have variable composition. NYS Regents items distinguish pure substances from mixtures by composition consistency and by whether the components can be separated by physical means.

questions.prompt: Combination of two or more substances in variable proportions where each substance retains its own identity.
questions.explanation: A mixture is a physical combination of two or more substances in variable proportions, where each substance keeps its chemical identity. Mixtures can be separated by physical means: filtration (solid-liquid), distillation (different boiling points), chromatography, evaporation, magnetic separation. Air, salt water, sand-iron, and orange juice are all mixtures. Unlike compounds, mixtures have no fixed ratio—salt water can be more or less salty. NYS Regents items distinguish mixtures from compounds by composition variability and by separation method (physical vs. chemical means).

questions.prompt: Mixture with uniform composition throughout, often called a solution; cannot distinguish components by sight.
questions.explanation: A homogeneous mixture has a uniform composition throughout, with components mixed at the molecular or ionic level so they cannot be distinguished by sight. Solutions are the most common type of homogeneous mixture: salt water (Na⁺, Cl⁻ ions dispersed in water), air (mainly N₂ and O₂), brass (Cu + Zn). Every sample of a homogeneous mixture has the same composition and properties. NYS Reference Table F ('Solubility Guidelines') helps predict whether ionic compounds dissolve to form homogeneous aqueous mixtures. Regents items often ask students to classify a substance as element, compound, homogeneous mixture, or heterogeneous mixture.

questions.prompt: Mixture with non-uniform composition; components can be distinguished and may settle out, like sand in water.
questions.explanation: A heterogeneous mixture has non-uniform composition: different parts of the sample have different properties, and components are visibly distinguishable. Examples include sand in water, oil and water (forms layers), salad, granite (visibly different mineral grains), and concrete. Heterogeneous mixtures can often be separated by simple physical methods like filtration, decanting, or settling. They contrast with homogeneous mixtures, where components are uniformly distributed at the molecular scale. NYS Regents items distinguish heterogeneous from homogeneous by visible uniformity and consistency of properties throughout the sample.

questions.prompt: Characteristic of matter that can be observed or measured without changing the substance's chemical identity, like density or melting point.
questions.explanation: A physical property can be observed or measured without changing the substance's chemical composition. Examples include color, density, melting point, boiling point, hardness, malleability, ductility, electrical conductivity, solubility, and state at room temperature. Physical properties are listed on NYS Reference Table S for elements and on Table H for vapor pressures. They contrast with chemical properties, which describe how a substance reacts with others (flammability, reactivity with acids, oxidation behavior). Regents items use physical-property tables to identify unknown substances or compare elements.

questions.prompt: Substance present in smaller amount in a solution; the one that gets dissolved.
questions.explanation: The solute is the substance dissolved in a solvent to form a solution. In saltwater, salt (NaCl) is the solute and water is the solvent. The solute can be solid (sugar in water), liquid (alcohol in water), or gas (CO₂ in soda). When two liquids are mixed, the smaller-quantity liquid is usually considered the solute. Solutes dissociate (ionic) or disperse (molecular) into individual particles during dissolution. NYS Reference Table F lists which ionic solutes are soluble in water. Regents items use the solute-solvent distinction in concentration calculations (molarity, ppm) and in solubility curve problems.

questions.prompt: Substance present in greater amount in a solution; the one that does the dissolving.
questions.explanation: The solvent is the substance that does the dissolving—usually present in greater quantity than the solute. Water is the most common solvent, called the 'universal solvent' because its polarity and hydrogen bonding let it dissolve a wide range of ionic and polar compounds. Other common solvents include ethanol (polar) and hexane (nonpolar). 'Like dissolves like': polar solvents dissolve polar/ionic solutes; nonpolar solvents dissolve nonpolar solutes. NYS Regents items often test whether a given combination forms a solution and ask students to identify the solvent by relative quantity or by polarity match.

questions.prompt: Solution in which water is the solvent; indicated by the symbol (aq) in chemical equations.
questions.explanation: An aqueous solution has water as the solvent. The notation (aq) appears in chemical equations to indicate ions or molecules dissolved in water: NaCl(s) → Na⁺(aq) + Cl⁻(aq). NYS Reference Table F ('Solubility Guidelines for Aqueous Solutions') helps students predict which ionic compounds will form (aq) solutions and which will form solid precipitates (s). Water dissolves most ionic compounds and many polar molecular compounds because of its polarity and hydrogen-bonding ability. Regents items use Table F constantly: every double-replacement, neutralization, and precipitation problem references aqueous solubility rules.

questions.prompt: Solution containing the maximum amount of dissolved solute at a given temperature; lies on the Reference Table G curve.
questions.explanation: A saturated solution contains the maximum amount of solute that will dissolve at a given temperature—any additional solute remains undissolved. The dissolved-undissolved ratio represents a dynamic equilibrium between dissolution and crystallization. On NYS Reference Table G ('Solubility Curves'), a point lying directly on a substance's curve represents a saturated solution. Above the curve = supersaturated (unstable, will crystallize); below = unsaturated. For example, at 50°C, water saturates with KNO₃ at about 85 g per 100 g H₂O. Regents items use Table G to determine saturation status and the amount needed to reach saturation.

questions.prompt: Solution containing less than the maximum amount of solute; lies below the Reference Table G curve.
questions.explanation: An unsaturated solution contains less solute than its maximum capacity at that temperature—more solute could still dissolve. On NYS Reference Table G, an unsaturated solution corresponds to a point below the substance's solubility curve. For example, 30 g of NaCl in 100 g of water at 50°C is unsaturated because the curve shows ~38 g could dissolve. Unsaturated solutions are far more common in everyday life than saturated ones: weak coffee, dilute juice, and lake water are all unsaturated with respect to their dissolved solids. Regents items use Table G to identify unsaturated/saturated/supersaturated solutions from coordinates.

questions.prompt: Concentration unit equal to moles of solute per liter of solution; abbreviated M.
questions.explanation: Molarity (M) is the most common concentration unit in chemistry: M = moles of solute / liters of solution. NYS Reference Table T includes the formula. A 1.0 M solution of NaCl contains 1.0 mol (58.5 g) of NaCl in enough water to make 1.0 L of solution. To prepare 500 mL of 0.2 M HCl, dissolve 0.1 mol HCl in water and dilute to 500 mL. Molarity changes with temperature (because volume changes), unlike molality. NYS Regents items use molarity for stoichiometry, dilution (M₁V₁ = M₂V₂), titration calculations, and converting between mass and volume of solution.

questions.prompt: Concentration unit useful for very dilute solutions, equal to grams of solute per million grams of solution.
questions.explanation: Parts per million (ppm) measures very low concentrations: ppm = (grams solute / grams solution) × 1,000,000. NYS Reference Table T includes the formula. 1 ppm is roughly 1 mg of solute per kg of solution—equivalent for water to 1 mg/L because water's density is ~1 g/mL. Drinking-water standards use ppm: EPA limits lead at 0.015 ppm; fluoride is added at 0.7-1.2 ppm. Pollution and trace-element analyses use ppm or ppb (parts per billion). NYS Regents items test ppm calculations for environmental and lab scenarios, and students must remember the factor of 1,000,000 in the denominator.

questions.prompt: Formula M₁V₁ = M₂V₂ used to calculate new concentration after adding more solvent to a solution.
questions.explanation: The dilution equation, M₁V₁ = M₂V₂, calculates how concentration changes when more solvent is added without changing the amount of solute. M₁ and V₁ are initial molarity and volume; M₂ and V₂ are final. The product MV represents moles of solute, which is constant during dilution. For example, diluting 50 mL of 6.0 M HCl to 300 mL gives a final concentration of (6.0)(50)/(300) = 1.0 M HCl. The equation applies because moles of solute don't change—only volume increases. NYS Regents items use dilution problems constantly, especially in titration setup and acid preparation. Volume units must match on both sides.

questions.prompt: Concentration unit equal to mass of solute divided by total mass of solution, multiplied by 100.
questions.explanation: Percent by mass (%m/m) = (grams solute / grams solution) × 100. NYS Reference Table T includes this formula. A 5% NaCl solution contains 5 g of NaCl in 100 g of total solution (5 g salt + 95 g water). Percent by mass is independent of temperature (unlike molarity) and is commonly used in commercial products: hydrogen peroxide is sold as 3% H₂O₂ (m/m). NYS Regents items use percent by mass especially for solution preparation problems and for distinguishing it from molarity. Students must remember to divide by total solution mass, not just solvent mass.

questions.prompt: Concentration unit equal to moles of solute per kilogram of solvent; independent of temperature.
questions.explanation: Molality (m) = moles of solute / kilograms of solvent (not solution). Unlike molarity, molality is temperature-independent because kilograms of solvent don't change with temperature—only volume does. Molality is preferred for colligative-property calculations: freezing-point depression (ΔTf = Kf·m) and boiling-point elevation (ΔTb = Kb·m). A 1.0 m solution of NaCl in water contains 1.0 mol (58.5 g) NaCl per 1.0 kg of water. Although NYS Chemistry Regents focuses more on molarity than molality, students should be able to distinguish the two by what they divide by: liters of solution (M) vs. kg of solvent (m).

questions.prompt: Graph showing how the maximum amount of solute that will dissolve changes with temperature; printed as Reference Table G.
questions.explanation: NYS Reference Table G ('Solubility Curves') plots solubility (g solute / 100 g H₂O) versus temperature for several common solutes: KNO₃, NaNO₃, NH₄Cl, KCl, NaCl, KClO₃, and the gases NH₃, HCl, SO₂. Most solid solutes increase in solubility with temperature (steep upward curves), while gases decrease with temperature (downward curves). Points on a curve = saturated; below = unsaturated; above = supersaturated. NYS Regents items use Table G to: identify the most/least soluble solute at a given temperature, determine grams that dissolve, find required temperature, and classify solutions by saturation.

questions.prompt: Effect describing how the solubility of most solids increases as the solution gets warmer; gases show the opposite trend.
questions.explanation: Temperature affects solubility differently for solids and gases. Most ionic and molecular solids dissolve more in hotter water—NYS Reference Table G shows steep upward curves for KNO₃ (~13 g/100 g at 0°C rising to ~210 g/100 g at 90°C). The increased kinetic energy of solvent molecules disrupts crystal lattices more effectively. Gases show the opposite trend: solubility decreases as temperature rises because faster-moving gas molecules escape solution—why warm soda fizzes more aggressively. Regents items use Table G to compare solubilities at different temperatures and to predict whether more or less solute can dissolve when heated.

questions.prompt: Effect that primarily changes the solubility of gases: increasing pressure increases the amount of gas dissolved.
questions.explanation: Pressure has little effect on solid or liquid solubility but strongly affects gas solubility. Increasing pressure forces more gas particles into solution—described quantitatively by Henry's law. Carbonated drinks contain CO₂ pressurized into solution; opening the bottle drops pressure, and CO₂ escapes as bubbles. Deep-sea divers experience increased gas solubility in their bloodstream at depth; rapid ascent causes 'the bends' as N₂ comes out of solution. Aquatic life depends on dissolved O₂, which varies with pressure and temperature. NYS Regents items use pressure effects in everyday contexts (soda, scuba) and in gas-solubility comparisons.

questions.prompt: Quantitative law stating that the solubility of a gas in a liquid is directly proportional to the partial pressure of that gas above the liquid.
questions.explanation: Henry's law (William Henry, 1803) states that the solubility of a gas in a liquid is directly proportional to the partial pressure of that gas above the liquid: S = kH·P, where kH is Henry's constant (different for each gas-solvent pair). Double the pressure → double the dissolved gas. The law explains carbonated beverages, dissolved oxygen in lakes, and decompression sickness. Henry's law assumes the gas doesn't react chemically with the solvent. While NYS Regents items don't usually require Henry's-law calculations, they do test the qualitative principle that higher pressure → more dissolved gas.

questions.prompt: Process by which dissolved particles come out of solution and form an organized solid; reverse of dissolving.
