questions.explanation: Crystallization is the formation of an organized solid from solution—the reverse of dissolution. It can occur by evaporating solvent, cooling a saturated solution, or seeding a supersaturated solution. In a saturated solution at equilibrium, dissolution and crystallization rates are equal. Slow cooling produces large, well-formed crystals (used in gemstone formation, sugar candy making, and protein crystallography); rapid cooling produces small crystals or amorphous solids. Above a solubility curve on NYS Reference Table G, a supersaturated solution is unstable and will crystallize. Regents items use crystallization in solubility-curve problems and to explain how evaporation salt forms.

questions.prompt: Substance that conducts electricity when dissolved in water because it produces ions.
questions.explanation: An electrolyte is a substance that dissociates into ions when dissolved in water, allowing the solution to conduct electricity. Strong electrolytes (NaCl, HCl, NaOH) ionize completely; weak electrolytes (acetic acid, ammonia) ionize only partially. Conductivity depends on ion concentration. Svante Arrhenius proposed in 1884 that electrolytes dissociate into ions in solution—a key insight underlying his Nobel Prize. NYS Reference Table F lists soluble ionic compounds, all of which are electrolytes when dissolved. Regents items test electrolyte identification by predicting whether a solution conducts (bulb lights vs. doesn't).

questions.prompt: Substance that does not produce ions in water and therefore does not conduct electricity in solution.
questions.explanation: A nonelectrolyte is a substance that dissolves in water but does not produce ions—molecular compounds that stay intact as neutral molecules. Sugar (C₁₂H₂₂O₁₁), ethanol (C₂H₅OH), and most other organic compounds are nonelectrolytes. Their aqueous solutions don't conduct electricity because no charged particles are present. Contrast with strong electrolytes (NaCl, HCl) that fully ionize. NYS Reference Table F distinguishes soluble ionic compounds (electrolytes) from molecular substances (which may dissolve without ionizing). Regents items use conductivity tests to identify whether a dissolved substance is an electrolyte or nonelectrolyte.

questions.prompt: Process by which an ionic compound separates into its component ions when dissolved in water.
questions.explanation: Dissociation is the separation of an ionic compound into its component ions when dissolved in water. NaCl(s) dissociates into Na⁺(aq) + Cl⁻(aq); CaCl₂ dissociates into Ca²⁺(aq) + 2Cl⁻(aq). Water molecules surround each ion through ion-dipole interactions, with O facing positive ions and H facing negative ions. Soluble ionic compounds (NYS Reference Table F) dissociate completely, making them strong electrolytes. Insoluble ionic compounds remain as solids and don't dissociate appreciably. NYS Regents items use dissociation equations in writing net ionic equations, in identifying spectator ions, and in concentration calculations.

questions.prompt: Process in which a molecular substance reacts with water to produce ions; how acids release H⁺.
questions.explanation: Ionization is the process by which a neutral molecular substance produces ions in solution. Unlike dissociation (where preexisting ions separate), ionization involves bond breaking and ion formation. HCl(g) ionizes in water: HCl + H₂O → H₃O⁺ + Cl⁻—a strong-acid example where ionization is essentially complete. Weak acids like acetic acid (CH₃COOH) ionize only partially, producing an equilibrium between molecules and ions. Arrhenius first proposed the ionization theory of acids and bases in 1884. NYS Regents items distinguish ionization (acids in water) from dissociation (ionic compounds dissolving) and test the equations involved.

questions.prompt: Lab procedure using a light bulb or meter to determine whether a solution contains ions; bright bulb = strong electrolyte.
questions.explanation: A conductivity test measures whether a solution can carry electric current. The setup connects two electrodes through the solution to a light bulb or meter. A bright bulb indicates a strong electrolyte (many ions, e.g., NaCl(aq), HCl(aq)); a dim bulb indicates a weak electrolyte (few ions, e.g., acetic acid); no light indicates a nonelectrolyte (no ions, e.g., sugar(aq) or distilled water). The test demonstrates Arrhenius's 1884 ion theory directly. NYS Regents items use conductivity tests to identify electrolyte strength, distinguish solution types, and connect ion concentration to conductivity.

questions.prompt: Increase in the boiling point of a solvent when a non-volatile solute is added; depends on the number of dissolved particles.
questions.explanation: Boiling-point elevation is a colligative property: adding a non-volatile solute raises the solvent's boiling point above its pure value. The magnitude depends only on the number of solute particles, not their identity: ΔTb = i·Kb·m, where i is the van't Hoff factor (number of particles per formula unit), Kb is the molal boiling-point constant (0.512°C/m for water), and m is molality. Adding 1 mol NaCl (i=2) to 1 kg water raises boiling point by ~1°C. This explains why salt is added to pasta water. NYS Regents items test the qualitative principle and the dependence on particle count.

questions.prompt: Decrease in the freezing point of a solvent when a solute is added; why salt melts ice on roads.
questions.explanation: Freezing-point depression is a colligative property: adding a solute lowers the solvent's freezing point below its pure value. The magnitude depends only on the number of solute particles: ΔTf = i·Kf·m, where Kf for water is 1.86°C/m. Adding 1 mol of NaCl (i=2) to 1 kg of water depresses freezing point by ~3.7°C. This is why salt is spread on icy roads (lowering freezing point below current temperature), why antifreeze is added to car radiators, and why ocean water freezes at about -2°C rather than 0°C. NYS Regents items use this principle to compare freezing points of solutions and identify which lowers it most.

questions.prompt: Reduction in vapor pressure of a solvent when a non-volatile solute is added; described by Raoult's law.
questions.explanation: Vapor pressure lowering is a colligative property: when a non-volatile solute is added to a solvent, fewer solvent molecules can escape the surface to the vapor phase, lowering vapor pressure. Quantified by Raoult's law: P_solution = X_solvent · P°_solvent, where X is the mole fraction of solvent. This drives the related effects of boiling-point elevation (since a solution boils when vapor pressure equals atmospheric pressure, and that now requires a higher temperature) and freezing-point depression. NYS Reference Table H shows pure-liquid vapor pressures; Regents items test the qualitative principle that solutes reduce vapor pressure.

questions.prompt: Pressure required to stop osmosis (water flow through a semipermeable membrane from low to high solute concentration).
questions.explanation: Osmotic pressure is the pressure that must be applied to stop osmosis—the flow of solvent through a semipermeable membrane from a region of low solute concentration to high. Quantified as π = i·M·R·T (van't Hoff equation). Osmotic pressure is biologically critical: it drives water across cell membranes, plant root pressure, and dialysis. Isotonic saline (0.9% NaCl) matches blood's osmotic pressure to prevent red blood cells from shrinking (hypertonic) or bursting (hypotonic). Although osmotic pressure calculations are rare on NYS Regents, the qualitative concept appears in colligative-property questions and in biology connections.

questions.prompt: Variable that determines the magnitude of all colligative properties: how many dissolved solute particles are present per kilogram of solvent.
questions.explanation: Particle concentration—the number of dissolved particles per unit solvent—determines the magnitude of every colligative property (freezing-point depression, boiling-point elevation, vapor-pressure lowering, osmotic pressure). The van't Hoff factor (i) counts particles per formula unit: glucose i=1, NaCl i=2 (Na⁺ + Cl⁻), CaCl₂ i=3 (Ca²⁺ + 2Cl⁻). So 1 m CaCl₂ depresses freezing point three times as much as 1 m glucose. Strong electrolytes have higher i values than nonelectrolytes. NYS Regents items use particle counts to compare colligative effects across solutions: 'Which solution has the lowest freezing point?' is answered by maximum particle concentration.

questions.prompt: Speed at which reactants are converted to products; commonly measured as change in concentration per unit time.
questions.explanation: Reaction rate measures how fast a reaction proceeds, usually expressed as moles of reactant consumed (or product formed) per liter per second. Faster rates mean more product per unit time. Rate depends on temperature, concentration, surface area, catalyst presence, and the nature of the reactants. Collision theory predicts that rate increases when collisions are more frequent or more effective. NYS Reference Table I (heats of reaction) and Table T's relevant equations support rate problems. Regents items often ask students to identify which factor would increase a reaction rate or to read rate data from a graph.

questions.prompt: Minimum energy reactant particles must have for a successful collision to form products; the 'energy hill' on a potential energy diagram.
questions.explanation: Activation energy (Ea) is the minimum energy reactant particles must have to overcome the energy barrier and form products. On a potential energy diagram, Ea is the height from reactant level to the peak (activated complex). Reactions with low Ea proceed quickly; high Ea reactions are slow at room temperature. Adding a catalyst lowers Ea, increasing rate without changing reactants, products, or ΔH. Svante Arrhenius proposed the activation-energy concept in 1889. NYS Regents items use Ea on potential energy diagrams to identify forward vs. reverse activation energies and to explain temperature and catalyst effects on rate.

questions.prompt: Substance that speeds up a reaction by lowering activation energy but is not consumed in the reaction.
questions.explanation: A catalyst speeds up a reaction by providing an alternative pathway with lower activation energy—it is not consumed and appears unchanged at the end. Enzymes are biological catalysts. The platinum-rhodium catalysts in car catalytic converters speed up the oxidation of CO and unburned hydrocarbons. On a potential energy diagram, a catalyst lowers both forward and reverse activation energies equally, so it speeds both directions of a reversible reaction but does not shift equilibrium. NYS Regents items use catalysts in PE diagrams (drawing a lower peak) and to explain why some reactions proceed quickly at room temperature.

questions.prompt: Theory explaining reaction rate in terms of particle collisions: more collisions and more effective collisions mean a faster reaction.
questions.explanation: Collision theory states that reactions occur when reactant particles collide with sufficient energy (≥ Ea) and proper orientation. Not every collision produces a reaction—only effective collisions do. Increasing reactant concentration, surface area, or temperature increases collision frequency; raising temperature also increases the fraction of collisions with energy ≥ Ea (a much larger effect than just collision frequency). Catalysts lower Ea so more collisions clear the energy barrier. NYS Chemistry Regents uses collision theory to explain every rate-affecting factor: students must connect each factor (temperature, concentration, surface area, catalyst, pressure for gases) to its mechanism.

questions.prompt: Collision in which reactant particles have enough energy and the proper orientation to produce a chemical reaction.
questions.explanation: An effective collision satisfies two conditions: (1) total kinetic energy ≥ activation energy, and (2) proper geometric orientation so that bonds can break and form. Most collisions are ineffective—either too low-energy or poorly oriented. Raising temperature increases both the frequency and the effectiveness of collisions (a larger fraction now exceeds Ea). Higher concentration increases collision frequency. Catalysts lower the Ea threshold so more existing collisions become effective. NYS Regents items use effective collisions to explain why grinding a solid (more surface area) or stirring a solution (more contact) speeds reactions, and why temperature has such a powerful rate-boosting effect.

questions.prompt: Graph plotting potential energy versus reaction progress, showing activation energy, energy of reactants and products, and heat of reaction.
questions.explanation: A potential energy (PE) diagram plots PE on the y-axis versus reaction progress on the x-axis. Key features: PE of reactants (left), PE of products (right), activation energy (height from reactants to peak), activation energy of reverse reaction (peak to products), heat of reaction ΔH (products − reactants), and the activated complex at the peak. Exothermic reactions show products below reactants (ΔH < 0); endothermic shows products above (ΔH > 0). NYS Reference Table I lists heats of reaction. Regents items routinely test reading PE diagrams: identifying Ea, Ea(reverse), ΔH, and the effect of a catalyst (lower peak).

questions.prompt: Reaction that releases heat to the surroundings; products have lower potential energy than reactants and ΔH is negative.
questions.explanation: An exothermic reaction releases energy to the surroundings as heat, making the surroundings warmer. The products have lower PE than the reactants, so ΔH < 0. On the PE diagram, the product line sits below the reactant line. Combustion (CH₄ + 2O₂ → CO₂ + 2H₂O + heat), neutralization (HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O + heat), and freezing are all exothermic. NYS Reference Table I lists ΔH values for many reactions; negative values indicate exothermic. Regents items use the sign of ΔH and the shape of PE diagrams to classify reactions, and identify exothermic processes from everyday observations (warmth, light, fire).

questions.prompt: Reaction that absorbs heat from the surroundings; products have higher potential energy than reactants and ΔH is positive.
questions.explanation: An endothermic reaction absorbs energy from the surroundings, making them cooler. The products have higher PE than the reactants, so ΔH > 0. On the PE diagram, the product line sits above the reactant line. Melting ice, evaporation, photosynthesis (6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂), and the reaction in instant cold packs (NH₄NO₃ dissolving in water) are endothermic. NYS Reference Table I lists positive ΔH values for endothermic reactions. Regents items use ΔH > 0 and the PE diagram shape to classify reactions and to explain why a system feels cool during the change.

questions.prompt: Highest-energy unstable arrangement of atoms during a reaction, located at the peak of the potential energy diagram.
questions.explanation: The activated complex (or transition state) is the high-energy, unstable arrangement of atoms at the peak of the PE diagram—the moment when old bonds are breaking and new ones are forming simultaneously. It cannot be isolated because it represents a transition rather than a discrete species. Its energy above reactants equals the forward activation energy. The activated complex concept was formalized by Henry Eyring (1935) in transition-state theory. NYS Regents items use the activated complex to identify the peak position on a PE diagram and to distinguish it from intermediates (which can be isolated and sit in shallow PE wells).

questions.prompt: Net energy change for a chemical reaction, often abbreviated ΔH; equals products minus reactants on a PE diagram.
questions.explanation: The heat of reaction (ΔH) is the difference in potential energy between products and reactants: ΔH = H(products) − H(reactants). A negative value means exothermic; positive means endothermic. NYS Reference Table I lists ΔH for common reactions, including combustion of methane (−890.4 kJ/mol). ΔH is independent of pathway (Hess's law) and is unaffected by catalysts (which change only the activation energy). On a PE diagram, ΔH equals the vertical distance from reactant line to product line. Regents items use ΔH from Table I and from PE diagrams to classify reactions and to calculate heat released/absorbed for a given amount of reactant.

questions.prompt: Factor that increases reaction rate by giving particles more kinetic energy, making more collisions effective.
questions.explanation: Increasing temperature speeds up reaction rate dramatically—a rule of thumb says rate doubles or triples for every 10°C rise. Higher temperature means greater average kinetic energy, so a larger fraction of collisions exceeds the activation-energy threshold. Increased speed also raises collision frequency, but the dominant effect is the activation-energy threshold. Arrhenius's 1889 rate equation k = A·exp(−Ea/RT) quantifies this: rate constant k grows exponentially with T. NYS Regents items use temperature increase to explain why food spoils slowly in a refrigerator and quickly at room temperature, and how cooking accelerates reactions.

questions.prompt: Factor that speeds up a reaction by exposing more reactant particles to collisions; powdered solids react faster than chunks.
questions.explanation: Increasing surface area speeds up reactions involving solids by exposing more particles to potential collisions. A finely powdered solid presents far more surface area than a single chunk of the same mass. This is why grain elevators must control dust (fine flour particles can ignite explosively), why chewing food helps digestion (smaller particles for enzymes to attack), and why steel wool burns faster than a steel ingot. The effect is dramatic only for heterogeneous reactions involving solids; homogeneous (all-aqueous or all-gas) reactions show no surface-area dependence. NYS Regents items use surface area to explain rate differences in solid-liquid or solid-gas reactions.

questions.prompt: Factor that speeds up reactions by providing more reactant particles per unit volume, leading to more frequent collisions.
questions.explanation: Increasing the concentration of reactants speeds up reactions in homogeneous systems (solutions or gases) by raising collision frequency. More reactant particles per unit volume mean more collisions per second. For aqueous solutions, doubling molarity of a reactant often roughly doubles the reaction rate (depending on rate law). For gases, increasing pressure has the same effect because it squeezes more particles into the same volume. NYS Regents items use concentration to explain why dilute acids react more slowly than concentrated ones and why reactions slow as reactants are consumed.

questions.prompt: Factor that speeds up gas-phase reactions by forcing molecules closer together, increasing collision frequency.
questions.explanation: Increasing pressure speeds up gas-phase reactions by reducing volume and thereby raising gas particle concentration. This makes collisions more frequent. Boyle's law (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂ at constant T) shows that doubling pressure halves volume, doubling gas concentration. Pressure changes have negligible effect on liquid or solid reactants (those are nearly incompressible). At equilibrium for a gas reaction with different numbers of moles on each side, pressure changes also shift the equilibrium position (Le Chatelier's principle). NYS Regents items use pressure on gas-phase reactions in rate problems and equilibrium shifts.

questions.prompt: Substance that slows down a reaction, opposite of a catalyst; can be added intentionally (preservatives) or unintentionally (poisons).
questions.explanation: An inhibitor slows or stops a reaction, opposite of a catalyst. Inhibitors work by raising effective activation energy or by binding to catalysts/enzymes. Food preservatives (BHT, sodium benzoate) inhibit spoilage; antifreeze inhibitors slow corrosion; many medications act as enzyme inhibitors (aspirin inhibits cyclooxygenase). 'Catalyst poisoning' occurs when an inhibitor binds irreversibly to a catalyst's active site, deactivating it. In industrial chemistry, inhibitors extend shelf life and prevent unwanted reactions. NYS Regents items use inhibitors to contrast with catalysts and to explain why some reactions can be slowed deliberately.

questions.prompt: State in which forward and reverse reaction rates are equal, producing no net change in concentrations even though both reactions continue.
questions.explanation: Dynamic equilibrium describes a reversible reaction in which forward and reverse rates are equal, so concentrations of reactants and products stay constant—but both reactions continue to occur. The system is 'dynamic' because particles continuously convert in both directions; it is 'in equilibrium' because the rates match. Equilibrium does not mean equal concentrations of reactants and products, only equal rates. Equilibrium can be established only in a closed system. NYS Regents items use the equality of forward and reverse rates to identify equilibrium and to explain why concentrations remain constant despite ongoing reaction.

questions.prompt: Numerical value (often called K or Keq) that describes the ratio of products to reactants at equilibrium for a particular reaction.
questions.explanation: The equilibrium constant (K or Keq) is the ratio of product concentrations to reactant concentrations at equilibrium, each raised to its stoichiometric coefficient. For aA + bB ⇌ cC + dD: K = [C]^c[D]^d / [A]^a[B]^b. K depends only on temperature, not on initial concentrations or amounts. K >> 1 means products dominate at equilibrium; K << 1 means reactants dominate. K = 1 indicates roughly equal amounts. While NYS Chemistry Regents focuses on qualitative Le Chatelier shifts rather than K calculations, students should understand that K characterizes the position of equilibrium and changes with temperature.

questions.prompt: Reaction proceeding from reactants to products; written left-to-right in a chemical equation.
questions.explanation: The forward reaction is the conversion of reactants (on the left of the equation) into products (on the right). In a reversible reaction A + B ⇌ C + D, the forward direction is A + B → C + D. Forward rate depends on reactant concentrations, temperature, and the presence of catalysts. At equilibrium, the forward rate equals the reverse rate, so net concentrations remain constant. The forward reaction continues even at equilibrium—equilibrium is dynamic, not static. NYS Regents items use the forward direction in Le Chatelier shifts: a stress that increases reactants increases the forward rate, shifting equilibrium toward products.

questions.prompt: Reaction proceeding from products back to reactants; written right-to-left in a reversible chemical equation.
questions.explanation: The reverse reaction converts products back to reactants—written right-to-left in a reversible equation. In A + B ⇌ C + D, the reverse direction is C + D → A + B. At the start of a reaction with only reactants present, the reverse rate is zero; it grows as products accumulate. Equilibrium is reached when forward rate = reverse rate. The reverse activation energy can be read on a PE diagram as the height from products to peak; Ea(reverse) = Ea(forward) − ΔH for exothermic reactions. NYS Regents items use the reverse reaction in Le Chatelier shifts: removing products increases the forward rate; adding products increases the reverse rate.

questions.prompt: System with no matter exchange with surroundings; required for chemical equilibrium to be established.
questions.explanation: A closed system allows no matter to enter or leave but may exchange energy with the surroundings. A sealed flask containing a reversible reaction is a closed system; an open beaker is not. Equilibrium can only be reached in a closed system because matter loss (e.g., a gas escaping) prevents the reverse reaction from balancing the forward one. A sealed bottle of carbonated water reaches equilibrium between dissolved CO₂ and gas-phase CO₂; an open bottle never reaches equilibrium because CO₂ escapes. NYS Regents items use the closed-system requirement to explain why equilibrium fails to develop in open containers and why Le Chatelier shifts assume a closed system.

questions.prompt: Anything that disturbs a system at equilibrium, such as a change in concentration, temperature, or pressure.
questions.explanation: A stress on an equilibrium system is any disturbance that changes the conditions and shifts the equilibrium position. Common stresses include adding or removing a reactant/product (concentration change), changing temperature, or changing pressure (for gas reactions with different mole counts). Le Chatelier's principle (Henry Louis Le Chatelier, 1884) states that the system responds to a stress by shifting in the direction that counteracts the stress. Catalysts do NOT stress the system—they speed both directions equally and don't change equilibrium position. NYS Regents items test which actions count as stresses and the direction of the resulting shift.

questions.prompt: Shift in equilibrium caused by adding or removing a reactant or product; opposite of the change is favored.
questions.explanation: A concentration shift occurs when reactant or product concentrations change. Adding more reactant shifts equilibrium toward products (forward); adding more product shifts toward reactants (reverse). Removing a reactant shifts the system in the reverse direction to replace it; removing a product shifts forward. For N₂ + 3H₂ ⇌ 2NH₃, adding N₂ pushes equilibrium toward NH₃; removing NH₃ also pushes equilibrium toward more NH₃. NYS Regents items use Le Chatelier to predict shift direction when a substance is added or removed and to explain how industrial processes (Haber-Bosch) maximize product yield by removing products.

questions.prompt: Shift in equilibrium caused by temperature change; for exothermic reactions, increasing temperature shifts the equilibrium toward reactants.
questions.explanation: A temperature shift treats heat as if it were a reactant or product. For an exothermic reaction (heat is a product), increasing temperature shifts equilibrium toward reactants—decreasing product yield. For an endothermic reaction (heat is a reactant), increasing temperature shifts toward products—increasing yield. Cooling does the opposite. Unlike concentration changes, temperature changes also change the equilibrium constant K. This is why industrial syntheses of ammonia (Haber process, exothermic) use compromise temperatures: lower T favors product but slows rate; higher T speeds rate but lowers yield. NYS Regents items use temperature shifts on exothermic and endothermic systems.

questions.prompt: Shift in equilibrium for gas-phase reactions caused by changing volume or pressure; system moves toward the side with fewer moles of gas when pressure is increased.
questions.explanation: A pressure shift affects only gas-phase reactions where the number of moles differs on each side. Increasing pressure (decreasing volume) shifts equilibrium toward the side with fewer moles of gas to reduce total particle count. For N₂(g) + 3H₂(g) ⇌ 2NH₃(g): 4 mol → 2 mol; high pressure favors NH₃. The Haber process exploits this with pressures of 200-300 atm. If both sides have equal moles, pressure has no effect. Liquids and solids are unaffected by pressure. NYS Regents items use mole-counting to predict the direction of pressure-induced shifts in gas-phase equilibria.

questions.prompt: Decrease in the solubility of a weak electrolyte caused by the addition of a strong electrolyte that shares an ion with it.
questions.explanation: The common ion effect describes how adding a strong electrolyte containing an ion already in solution decreases the solubility of a weak electrolyte that shares that ion. For example, adding NaCl to a saturated AgCl solution decreases AgCl solubility because the added Cl⁻ shifts the dissolution equilibrium AgCl(s) ⇌ Ag⁺(aq) + Cl⁻(aq) toward solid (left). The effect is a direct application of Le Chatelier's principle to a saturated solution. The common ion effect is exploited in qualitative analysis to precipitate selected ions. NYS Regents items may use the common ion effect to predict whether a precipitate forms or dissolves.

questions.prompt: 1884 definition of an acid as a substance that produces hydrogen ions (H⁺) when dissolved in water.
questions.explanation: Svante Arrhenius proposed in 1884 that an acid is any substance that ionizes in water to produce H⁺ (hydrogen ions, or protons). HCl(aq) → H⁺(aq) + Cl⁻(aq) is the classic Arrhenius acid ionization. Arrhenius's ionization theory won him the 1903 Nobel Prize in Chemistry. NYS Reference Table K lists common Arrhenius acids (HCl, HNO₃, H₂SO₄, HC₂H₃O₂, H₃PO₄, H₂CO₃). The H⁺ ion in water actually exists as H₃O⁺ (hydronium), a refinement that came later, but the Arrhenius definition is still the foundation of NYS Regents acid-base chemistry. Regents items use Table K to identify acids.

questions.prompt: 1884 definition of a base as a substance that produces hydroxide ions (OH⁻) when dissolved in water.
questions.explanation: Svante Arrhenius defined a base in 1884 as a substance that produces OH⁻ (hydroxide ions) when dissolved in water. NaOH(s) → Na⁺(aq) + OH⁻(aq) is a classic Arrhenius base dissociation. NYS Reference Table L lists common Arrhenius bases (NaOH, KOH, Ca(OH)₂, NH₃·H₂O). Like the Arrhenius acid definition, this concept won the 1903 Nobel Prize. The Arrhenius framework is limited to aqueous solutions but works for almost everything in NYS Chemistry. Regents items use Table L to identify bases and to predict neutralization products (acid + base → salt + water).

questions.prompt: 1923 definition of a base as a proton (H⁺) acceptor; broader than the Arrhenius hydroxide definition.
questions.explanation: Brønsted and Lowry (1923) defined a base as a proton (H⁺) acceptor. The water molecule itself can act as a Brønsted-Lowry base by accepting a proton to form H₃O⁺. Ammonia (NH₃) is a B-L base: NH₃ + H₂O → NH₄⁺ + OH⁻, where NH₃ accepts a proton from H₂O. The conjugate acid of a B-L base is the species formed after accepting a proton: NH₃'s conjugate acid is NH₄⁺. The B-L framework is more general than Arrhenius's hydroxide-focused definition. NYS Regents items use the B-L definition for proton acceptors and for identifying conjugate acid-base pairs.

questions.prompt: Substance that can act as either an acid or a base, depending on the reaction partner; water is the classic example.
questions.explanation: An amphoteric (or amphiprotic) substance can either donate or accept a proton, depending on the partner. Water is the textbook example: with HCl, water acts as a base (H₂O + HCl → H₃O⁺ + Cl⁻); with NH₃, water acts as an acid (H₂O + NH₃ → OH⁻ + NH₄⁺). The autoionization of water produces small equal amounts of H₃O⁺ and OH⁻ ions (Kw = 10⁻¹⁴ at 25°C), defining pH 7 as neutral. Other amphoteric substances include amino acids (with -NH₂ and -COOH groups), HSO₄⁻ (can donate or accept H⁺), and Al(OH)₃. NYS Regents items use amphoteric substances especially for water's dual role.

questions.prompt: Numerical scale (0 to 14) measuring the concentration of hydrogen ions in a solution; defined as −log[H⁺].
questions.explanation: The pH scale, introduced by Søren Sørensen in 1909, runs from 0 (most acidic) through 7 (neutral) to 14 (most basic). Mathematically, pH = −log[H⁺] (or equivalently −log[H₃O⁺]). A pH of 3 means [H⁺] = 10⁻³ M; pH 10 means [H⁺] = 10⁻¹⁰ M. Each pH unit represents a 10× change in H⁺ concentration: pH 3 has 100 times more H⁺ than pH 5. NYS Chemistry Regents focuses on the qualitative pH scale: acids pH < 7, neutral pH = 7, bases pH > 7. Reference Table M lists indicators with their pH ranges. Regents items test pH classification, indicator color, and the 10× factor between pH units.

questions.prompt: Positively charged ion formed when an acid donates a proton to water; formula H₃O⁺.
questions.explanation: The hydronium ion (H₃O⁺) forms when an H⁺ from an acid attaches to a water molecule: HCl + H₂O → H₃O⁺ + Cl⁻. H₃O⁺ is the actual form that acidic hydrogen takes in aqueous solution; the bare H⁺ doesn't exist alone in water. The concentration of H₃O⁺ determines pH. In neutral water at 25°C, [H₃O⁺] = [OH⁻] = 10⁻⁷ M, giving pH 7. Acids increase [H₃O⁺]; bases decrease it. NYS Regents items often use H₃O⁺ interchangeably with H⁺ in acid ionization equations. Identifying H₃O⁺ as the proton donor in Brønsted-Lowry reactions is a common Regents skill.

questions.prompt: Negatively charged ion released when a base dissociates; formula OH⁻.
questions.explanation: The hydroxide ion (OH⁻) is released by Arrhenius bases dissolving in water: NaOH(s) → Na⁺(aq) + OH⁻(aq). OH⁻ concentration determines basicity. In neutral water, [OH⁻] = 10⁻⁷ M. The product [H₃O⁺][OH⁻] always equals 10⁻¹⁴ at 25°C (Kw), so increasing one decreases the other. The pOH scale parallels pH: pOH = −log[OH⁻]; pH + pOH = 14 at 25°C. Strong bases (NaOH, KOH) dissociate completely; weak bases (NH₃·H₂O) ionize only partially. NYS Reference Table L lists common bases. Regents items use OH⁻ in dissociation equations and neutralization reactions (H⁺ + OH⁻ → H₂O).

questions.prompt: Substance that changes color in response to pH, used to estimate the acidity or basicity of a solution.
questions.explanation: An acid-base indicator is a weak acid or base whose conjugate forms have different colors. As pH changes, the indicator shifts between its acid form (one color) and conjugate base form (another color). NYS Reference Table M ('Common Acid-Base Indicators') lists six indicators with their pH ranges: methyl orange (3.1-4.4, red→yellow), bromthymol blue (6.0-7.6, yellow→blue), phenolphthalein (8.2-10, colorless→pink), litmus (4.5-8.3, red→blue), bromcresol green (3.8-5.4, yellow→blue), and thymol blue (8.0-9.6, yellow→blue). Regents items use Table M to predict indicator color at a given pH and to select an appropriate indicator for a titration.

questions.prompt: Paper strip that turns red in acidic solutions and blue in basic solutions; a classic chemistry indicator.
questions.explanation: Litmus paper, made from lichen-derived dye, is the oldest acid-base indicator (used since the 14th century). Red litmus turns blue in basic solutions (pH ~8 and above); blue litmus turns red in acidic solutions (pH ~4 and below). Litmus paper provides a qualitative pH classification (acidic/basic/neutral) but cannot give a numerical pH. NYS Reference Table M lists litmus with a transition range of 4.5-8.3 (red below 4.5, blue above 8.3, purple in between). Regents items use litmus to identify whether an unknown solution is acidic or basic and to compare with more precise indicators like phenolphthalein or methyl orange.

questions.prompt: Chemical reaction between an acid and a base producing a salt and water; classic double-replacement reaction.
questions.explanation: A neutralization reaction occurs when an acid and a base combine to form a salt and water. The general equation is: acid + base → salt + water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O. The net ionic equation simplifies to H⁺ + OH⁻ → H₂O, since Na⁺ and Cl⁻ are spectator ions. Neutralization is highly exothermic (ΔH ≈ −56 kJ/mol of water formed) and is used to treat acid spills, indigestion (TUMS contain CaCO₃), and acid mine drainage. NYS Regents items use neutralization to predict products, balance equations, write net ionic equations, and calculate concentrations via titration.

questions.prompt: Ionic compound produced when an acid neutralizes a base; consists of a cation from the base and an anion from the acid.
questions.explanation: A salt is an ionic compound formed in a neutralization reaction, consisting of the metal cation from the base and the nonmetal/polyatomic anion from the acid. HCl + NaOH → NaCl (table salt) + H₂O. H₂SO₄ + 2KOH → K₂SO₄ + 2H₂O. Salts are produced in many other reactions too (e.g., metal + acid, metal oxide + acid). They are typically water-soluble (NYS Reference Table F) and form crystalline solids. Salts may be neutral, acidic (NH₄Cl), or basic (Na₂CO₃) depending on the parent acid/base strengths. NYS Regents items use salts to identify neutralization products, write balanced equations, and apply Table F solubility rules.

questions.prompt: Liquid product always formed alongside the salt in a neutralization reaction; molecular formula H₂O.
questions.explanation: Water (H₂O) is always produced in acid-base neutralization, formed by the combination of H⁺ from the acid and OH⁻ from the base. The net ionic equation H⁺ + OH⁻ → H₂O captures this universal step. The formation of water is highly exothermic (ΔH ≈ −56 kJ/mol) because the resulting O-H bonds are very stable. Even in reactions where the salt remains dissolved (NaCl), water formation is the underlying chemistry. NYS Regents items use water as a guaranteed product in all neutralization reactions and as part of the balanced equation. Identifying water as a product helps students predict neutralization outcomes.

questions.prompt: Lab procedure using a buret to gradually add an acid or base of known concentration to a solution of unknown concentration to determine the unknown.
questions.explanation: Titration is a quantitative analytical technique used to determine the concentration of an unknown acid or base. A buret delivers titrant of known concentration into a flask containing the unknown plus a few drops of indicator. The volume of titrant added when the indicator changes color marks the endpoint, which is close to the equivalence point (mol H⁺ = mol OH⁻). The titration equation on NYS Reference Table T is M_A V_A = M_B V_B (for monoprotic acid and monobasic base). Knowing three of M_A, V_A, M_B, V_B lets students solve for the fourth. Regents items use titration in calculation problems and conceptual questions about endpoint indicators.

questions.prompt: Point in a titration when stoichiometrically equal moles of acid and base have been combined.
questions.explanation: The equivalence point (or stoichiometric point) of a titration is when the moles of acid added equals the moles of base added (in the right stoichiometric ratio). For a monoprotic acid and monobasic base, M_A V_A = M_B V_B at the equivalence point. The pH at the equivalence point depends on the acid/base strengths: strong-acid + strong-base gives pH 7; weak acid + strong base gives pH > 7; strong acid + weak base gives pH < 7. The endpoint (when the indicator changes color) is chosen close to the equivalence point but is not always exactly the same. NYS Regents items use the titration equation to calculate concentrations at the equivalence point.

questions.prompt: Acid that ionizes completely in water, producing a high concentration of hydrogen ions and a low pH.
questions.explanation: A strong acid ionizes completely in water—every molecule dissociates into H⁺ and a conjugate base. The six common strong acids on NYS Reference Table K are HCl, HBr, HI, HNO₃, HClO₄, and H₂SO₄ (first ionization). A 0.1 M HCl solution has [H⁺] ≈ 0.1 M, giving pH ≈ 1. Strong acids fully conduct electricity (strong electrolytes), react vigorously with metals, and are corrosive. Strength (extent of ionization) is different from concentration (amount of acid in a given volume): a dilute strong acid can be less corrosive than a concentrated weak acid. NYS Regents items distinguish strength from concentration carefully.

questions.prompt: Acid that ionizes only partially in water; most acetic acid molecules in vinegar remain unionized.
questions.explanation: A weak acid ionizes only partially in water, with most molecules remaining intact. Acetic acid (CH₃COOH in vinegar) is about 1% ionized in 0.1 M solution: only 1 of every 100 molecules releases H⁺. Other weak acids include carbonic acid (H₂CO₃), phosphoric acid (H₃PO₄), and HF. Weak acids have higher pH than strong acids at the same concentration (a 0.1 M acetic acid solution has pH ≈ 2.9, vs. pH 1 for 0.1 M HCl) and conduct electricity weakly. The undissociated molecule and the H⁺/conjugate base exist in equilibrium. NYS Regents items distinguish strong from weak acids using Table K and equilibrium ideas.

questions.prompt: Base that dissociates completely in water, producing a high concentration of hydroxide ions and a high pH.
questions.explanation: A strong base dissociates completely in water, releasing all of its OH⁻ ions. The common strong bases on NYS Reference Table L are the Group 1 hydroxides (LiOH, NaOH, KOH, RbOH, CsOH) and heavier Group 2 hydroxides (Ca(OH)₂, Sr(OH)₂, Ba(OH)₂). A 0.1 M NaOH solution has [OH⁻] ≈ 0.1 M and pH ≈ 13. Strong bases are caustic (damage skin and tissues), strong electrolytes, and react vigorously with acids. Like strong acids, strong bases have a strength (extent of dissociation) distinct from concentration. NYS Regents items use Table L to identify strong bases and to predict pH values.

questions.prompt: Base that produces only a small concentration of hydroxide ions in water; ammonia is the classic example.
questions.explanation: A weak base ionizes only partially in water. Ammonia (NH₃) is the most common weak base: NH₃ + H₂O ⇌ NH₄⁺ + OH⁻, with only about 1% ionization in 0.1 M solution. The pH of 0.1 M ammonia is about 11.1 (vs. pH 13 for 0.1 M NaOH). Other weak bases include the carbonate ion (CO₃²⁻) and most amines. Weak bases are weaker electrolytes than strong bases. NYS Reference Table L lists ammonia. Regents items distinguish weak from strong bases by extent of ionization and by pH at equivalent concentrations.

questions.prompt: Fraction of acid or base molecules that have ionized; close to 100% for strong electrolytes, close to 0% for weak ones.
questions.explanation: Percent dissociation (or percent ionization) measures the fraction of molecules that have ionized in solution: % = (ionized concentration / initial concentration) × 100. Strong acids and bases ionize nearly 100%; weak acids and bases ionize 1-5% typically. For acetic acid (a weak acid), 0.1 M solution shows about 1.3% dissociation. Percent dissociation depends on both the strength of the acid/base AND the concentration: weak acids dissociate more (as a percent) when diluted. NYS Regents items use this concept qualitatively—distinguishing strong (~100%) from weak (<5%) electrolytes—even when the exact percent isn't calculated.

questions.prompt: Substance listed on Reference Table M that changes color over a specific pH range.
questions.explanation: NYS Reference Table M ('Common Acid-Base Indicators') tabulates six indicators with their pH transition ranges and color changes: methyl orange (3.1-4.4, red→yellow), bromthymol blue (6.0-7.6, yellow→blue), phenolphthalein (8.2-10, colorless→pink), litmus (4.5-8.3, red→blue), bromcresol green (3.8-5.4, yellow→blue), and thymol blue (8.0-9.6, yellow→blue). For titrations, students choose an indicator whose pH range brackets the equivalence point. NYS Regents items use Table M to predict color at a given pH, to select titration indicators, and to estimate pH from observed indicator colors.

questions.prompt: Indicator that is colorless in acidic solutions and pink in basic solutions (pH range 8.2-10); commonly used for strong-acid/strong-base titrations.
questions.explanation: Phenolphthalein, discovered by Adolf von Baeyer in 1871, is the most-used indicator in strong-acid/strong-base titrations. Its NYS Reference Table M range is 8.2-10, colorless in acid (below 8.2) and bright pink/magenta in base (above 10). Because strong-acid/strong-base titrations have equivalence points near pH 7, phenolphthalein gives a sharp color change just past equivalence as the solution moves through pH 9-10. It is also a mild laxative (used historically in Ex-Lax). NYS Regents items use phenolphthalein to predict colors before and after titration and to identify the indicator's pH range from Table M.

questions.prompt: Indicator that is yellow below pH 6.0 and blue above pH 7.6, commonly used in fish-tank pH testing.
questions.explanation: Bromthymol blue (NYS Reference Table M range 6.0-7.6) is yellow in acid and blue in base, passing through green at neutral. Its transition range straddles pH 7, making it useful for testing solutions near neutral pH—commonly used in aquariums, swimming pools, and biology experiments showing CO₂ uptake by plants (CO₂ + water forms carbonic acid, turning the dye yellow). Photosynthesis removes CO₂, raising pH back to blue. NYS Regents items use bromthymol blue to identify pH or to select an indicator that changes color around neutral. Memorizing the colors and ranges from Table M is essential.

questions.prompt: Indicator that is red below pH 3.1 and yellow above pH 4.4, useful for titrations involving weak bases and strong acids.
questions.explanation: Methyl orange (NYS Reference Table M range 3.1-4.4) is red in strongly acidic solution and yellow above pH 4.4. Because its transition range sits well below pH 7, methyl orange is the indicator of choice for titrations where the equivalence point is acidic—for example, titrating a weak base (NH₃) with a strong acid (HCl), where the equivalence point falls near pH 5. Discovered in the 19th century, methyl orange is also used as a dye. NYS Regents items use methyl orange to identify pH ranges and predict colors at various pHs, often pairing it with phenolphthalein to demonstrate how different indicators cover different pH ranges.

questions.prompt: Color observed when the indicator changes during a titration; signals that the endpoint has been reached.
questions.explanation: The endpoint color is the indicator color at which a titration is considered complete—when the solution shows the indicator's transition color (typically a hue between the acid and base forms). For phenolphthalein, the endpoint is the first faint persistent pink. For methyl orange, it is the change from red to orange/yellow. The endpoint should be chosen so that it coincides with (or is very close to) the equivalence point, where moles of acid = moles of base. NYS Regents items use endpoint color to identify when to stop adding titrant and to verify that the indicator was appropriate for the given titration's expected pH.

questions.prompt: Assigned charge that tracks electron ownership in a compound; equal to ion charge for monatomic ions.
questions.explanation: Oxidation state (or oxidation number) is the hypothetical charge assigned to an atom assuming all bonds are ionic. Rules: free elements = 0 (O₂ = 0, Cu = 0); monatomic ions = their charge (Na⁺ = +1, Cl⁻ = −1); oxygen usually −2 (except in peroxides where it's −1); hydrogen usually +1 (except metal hydrides where it's −1); sum of oxidation states in a compound = total charge. For H₂SO₄: H is +1, O is −2, so S must be +6. NYS Periodic Table boxes list common oxidation states. Regents items use oxidation states to identify redox reactions, balance redox equations, and determine what's oxidized vs. reduced.

questions.prompt: Half-reaction in which a substance loses electrons, increasing its oxidation state.
questions.explanation: Oxidation is the loss of electrons—the oxidized substance's oxidation state increases. The mnemonic LEO ('Lose Electrons = Oxidation') helps students remember. Example: Zn → Zn²⁺ + 2e⁻ (zinc loses 2 electrons, oxidation state goes from 0 to +2). Oxidation always accompanies reduction in a redox reaction (they are coupled). The historical origin of the term 'oxidation' comes from reactions with oxygen (Mg + ½O₂ → MgO), but the modern definition is based on electron loss regardless of oxygen's presence. NYS Regents items use oxidation in writing half-reactions and identifying which species lost electrons.

questions.prompt: Half-reaction in which a substance gains electrons, decreasing its oxidation state.
questions.explanation: Reduction is the gain of electrons—the reduced substance's oxidation state decreases. The mnemonic GER ('Gain Electrons = Reduction') complements LEO. Example: Cu²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Cu (copper gains 2 electrons, oxidation state goes from +2 to 0). Reduction always accompanies oxidation in a redox reaction. The term 'reduction' historically described how metal ores were 'reduced' to pure metals by removing oxygen (e.g., Fe₂O₃ + 3CO → 2Fe + 3CO₂), reducing the metal's mass per unit volume. NYS Regents items use reduction in writing half-reactions and identifying species that gained electrons.

questions.prompt: Substance that causes another substance to be oxidized; it accepts electrons and is itself reduced.
questions.explanation: An oxidizing agent (or oxidant) is the substance that causes another to be oxidized by accepting electrons. The oxidizing agent is itself reduced in the process. Strong oxidizing agents include F₂ (the strongest), O₂, Cl₂, KMnO₄, K₂Cr₂O₇, and HNO₃. NYS Reference Table J (the activity series) ranks metals' tendencies to be oxidized—the more active a metal, the better a reducing agent it is, and the weaker its conjugate cation as an oxidizing agent. Regents items identify oxidizing agents by tracking which species was reduced (oxidation state decreased).

questions.prompt: Substance that causes another substance to be reduced; it donates electrons and is itself oxidized.
questions.explanation: A reducing agent (or reductant) is the substance that causes another to be reduced by donating electrons. The reducing agent is itself oxidized. Strong reducing agents include alkali and alkaline earth metals (Li, K, Na, Mg, Ca), H₂, and CO. NYS Reference Table J ranks metals' reducing strength: the most active metals (Li at top) are the strongest reducing agents and most easily oxidized. Regents items identify reducing agents by tracking which species was oxidized (oxidation state increased) and by using Table J to predict which of two metals would reduce the other.

questions.prompt: Equation showing just the oxidation or reduction part of a redox reaction, with electrons explicitly written.
questions.explanation: A half-reaction shows only the oxidation or the reduction part of a redox reaction, with electrons written explicitly. Example for Zn + Cu²⁺ → Zn²⁺ + Cu: Oxidation half-reaction: Zn → Zn²⁺ + 2e⁻; Reduction half-reaction: Cu²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Cu. The two half-reactions must be balanced so electrons lost = electrons gained when combined. Half-reactions are useful for analyzing each electron-transfer step separately and for predicting cell potentials from NYS Reference Table N. Regents items use half-reactions to identify which species is oxidized/reduced, to balance overall equations, and to construct voltaic cells.

questions.prompt: Movement of electrons from one species to another that defines every oxidation-reduction reaction.
questions.explanation: Electron transfer is the defining feature of redox: one species loses electrons (oxidation), another gains them (reduction). Electrons can be transferred directly (in a single beaker, like Zn dropped into CuSO₄) or through an external wire (in a voltaic cell). In a balanced redox equation, total electrons lost = total electrons gained. Tracking electrons through oxidation-state changes confirms whether a reaction is redox. NYS Regents items use electron-transfer counts to balance redox half-reactions, to identify the number of electrons in a balanced overall equation, and to distinguish redox from non-redox reactions (acid-base, precipitation).

questions.prompt: Final equation showing all reactants, products, and stoichiometric coefficients in a redox reaction, with charges balanced.
questions.explanation: A balanced redox equation has both mass and charge balanced. To balance: (1) write half-reactions, (2) balance atoms other than O and H, (3) balance O with H₂O, (4) balance H with H⁺, (5) balance charge with electrons, (6) multiply half-reactions so electrons cancel, (7) add and simplify. For Zn + Cu²⁺ → Zn²⁺ + Cu: oxidation Zn → Zn²⁺ + 2e⁻; reduction Cu²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Cu; sum: Zn + Cu²⁺ → Zn²⁺ + Cu (already balanced). NYS Regents items test redox balancing especially with multi-electron half-reactions and acidic-solution problems requiring H⁺ and H₂O.

questions.prompt: Ion that appears unchanged on both sides of a chemical equation; gets canceled when writing the net ionic equation.
questions.explanation: A spectator ion appears on both sides of an ionic equation in the same form, taking no part in the chemistry. In Zn(s) + CuSO₄(aq) → ZnSO₄(aq) + Cu(s), the SO₄²⁻ is a spectator: it just balances charge. The net ionic equation Zn + Cu²⁺ → Zn²⁺ + Cu omits SO₄²⁻. In acid-base neutralization HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O, Na⁺ and Cl⁻ are spectators; the net ionic equation is H⁺ + OH⁻ → H₂O. NYS Regents items use spectator ions to write net ionic equations and to identify the chemistry stripped of accompanying ions.

questions.prompt: Simplified equation showing only the ions and molecules that actually change during a reaction, with spectator ions removed.
questions.explanation: A net ionic equation shows only the species that participate in the chemical change, with spectator ions canceled out. To write one: (1) write the complete molecular equation, (2) write the complete ionic equation (all soluble strong electrolytes as ions), (3) cancel spectators. For AgNO₃(aq) + NaCl(aq) → AgCl(s) + NaNO₃(aq), the net ionic is Ag⁺ + Cl⁻ → AgCl(s); Na⁺ and NO₃⁻ are spectators. Net ionic equations highlight the essential chemistry. NYS Regents items use them in precipitation, neutralization, and redox to show the actual reacting species.

questions.prompt: Electrochemical cell that uses a spontaneous redox reaction to produce electrical energy; the chemistry inside batteries.
questions.explanation: A voltaic cell (also called a galvanic cell) converts chemical energy into electrical energy through a spontaneous redox reaction. Two electrodes (anode and cathode) sit in separate half-cells connected by a salt bridge and an external wire. Electrons flow from anode (oxidation) to cathode (reduction) through the wire; ions flow through the salt bridge to maintain charge neutrality. Italian Alessandro Volta built the first voltaic cell in 1800 (the 'voltaic pile' using zinc and copper disks). Modern batteries (alkaline, lithium-ion) are practical voltaic cells. NYS Reference Table J helps predict spontaneity. Regents items use voltaic cells to identify anode/cathode and electron flow.

questions.prompt: Electrochemical cell that uses electrical energy to drive a non-spontaneous redox reaction; used in electroplating.
questions.explanation: An electrolytic cell uses an external power source (battery) to force a non-spontaneous redox reaction. Examples include electroplating (depositing a thin metal layer on an object), electrolysis of water (2H₂O → 2H₂ + O₂), and electrolytic refining of copper. In electrolytic cells, the anode is positive (connected to the +terminal of the power source) and the cathode is negative—opposite of voltaic cells, because the power source drives the cell against its spontaneous direction. Industrial aluminum production via the Hall-Héroult process (1886) is electrolytic. NYS Regents items distinguish voltaic from electrolytic by spontaneity and electrode polarity.

questions.prompt: Electrode in an electrochemical cell where oxidation occurs; remember 'AN OX' to keep track.
questions.explanation: The anode is the electrode where oxidation occurs (mnemonic: 'AN OX'). In a voltaic cell, the anode has negative polarity—electrons leave it through the external wire. In an electrolytic cell, the anode has positive polarity (connected to + terminal). The anode is consumed in some cells (Zn anode in a zinc-copper voltaic cell wears away as Zn → Zn²⁺ + 2e⁻). In electroplating, the anode is often the source metal being deposited. NYS Regents items use the anode-oxidation rule to identify which half-reaction occurs at the anode and to predict electrode mass changes.

questions.prompt: Electrode in an electrochemical cell where reduction occurs; remember 'RED CAT' to keep track.
questions.explanation: The cathode is the electrode where reduction occurs (mnemonic: 'RED CAT'). In a voltaic cell, the cathode has positive polarity—electrons arrive there through the external wire. In an electrolytic cell, the cathode has negative polarity (connected to − terminal). Reduction at the cathode deposits material in many cells (Cu²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Cu plates onto the cathode in copper electroplating). NYS Regents items use the cathode-reduction rule to identify which half-reaction occurs at the cathode and to predict mass changes (cathode typically gains mass in electroplating cells).

questions.prompt: Tube or porous barrier in a voltaic cell that allows ions to flow between half-cells, maintaining electrical neutrality.
questions.explanation: A salt bridge is a tube containing an inert electrolyte (often KNO₃, NaCl, or KCl in agar) that connects the two half-cells of a voltaic cell. It allows ions to migrate between half-cells, completing the circuit and maintaining electrical neutrality as the redox reaction proceeds. Without the salt bridge, positive charge would build up at the anode (where oxidation produces cations) and negative charge at the cathode (where reduction consumes cations), quickly stopping the reaction. Cations from the salt bridge flow to the cathode; anions flow to the anode. NYS Regents items use the salt bridge to identify ion movement direction and explain cell function.

questions.prompt: Reference Table J ranking of metals by their tendency to be oxidized; more active metals can displace less active metal ions from solution.
questions.explanation: NYS Reference Table J ('Activity Series') lists metals in order of decreasing activity, from Li at the top (most easily oxidized) to Au at the bottom (least easily oxidized). A metal higher on the list can displace a metal lower on the list from its compound: Zn + CuSO₄ → ZnSO₄ + Cu (zinc is above copper). Hydrogen serves as the reference: metals above H react with acids to release H₂ gas; metals below H do not. Table J also lists nonmetals (F₂ most active down to I₂ least). Regents items use Table J to predict whether a single-replacement reaction occurs spontaneously.

questions.prompt: Voltage of a half-reaction relative to the standard hydrogen electrode; listed on Reference Table N for many half-reactions.
questions.explanation: Standard reduction potential (E°) is the voltage of a reduction half-reaction relative to the standard hydrogen electrode (assigned E° = 0.00 V). NYS Reference Table N (in some Regents editions) lists half-reactions and their E° values. More positive E° = stronger oxidizing agent (more readily reduced). To predict a voltaic cell's potential: E°cell = E°cathode − E°anode (both as reduction potentials). A positive E°cell indicates a spontaneous reaction. Although NYS Chemistry Regents typically tests qualitative use of activity series (Table J) rather than quantitative E° calculations, students should understand E° as a measure of reduction tendency.

questions.prompt: Reaction that proceeds in the forward direction without input of external energy; predicted by Table J or by positive E°cell.
questions.explanation: A spontaneous reaction proceeds forward without external energy input. In electrochemistry, a spontaneous redox reaction has E°cell > 0 (positive cell potential). Voltaic cells use spontaneous reactions to generate electricity; electrolytic cells use external energy to drive non-spontaneous reactions. NYS Reference Table J predicts single-replacement spontaneity: a metal above another in Table J will displace it spontaneously (Zn + CuSO₄ → ZnSO₄ + Cu, spontaneous because Zn is above Cu). Regents items use Table J to determine whether single-replacement reactions occur and to predict products when they do.

questions.prompt: Total voltage produced by a voltaic cell, measured in volts; equals the difference between cathode and anode reduction potentials.
questions.explanation: Cell potential (E°cell) is the voltage produced by a voltaic cell, measured in volts. Calculated as E°cell = E°cathode − E°anode (both as reduction potentials). A standard zinc-copper cell produces ~1.10 V (Cu²⁺/Cu = +0.34 V minus Zn²⁺/Zn = −0.76 V). A positive cell potential indicates spontaneous operation; negative indicates non-spontaneous (would need an electrolytic setup). The cell voltage decreases as reactants are consumed and equilibrium approaches. Although NYS Chemistry Regents focuses more on qualitative cell behavior than quantitative E° calculations, students may need to identify cell potential from a given diagram or table.

questions.prompt: Tendency of a metal to lose electrons and form a cation; correlates with position on Reference Table J.
questions.explanation: Metal reactivity measures how readily a metal loses electrons to form a cation—essentially, how easily it is oxidized. NYS Reference Table J ranks metals from most reactive (Li, K, Ca, Na, Mg, Al, Zn, Fe...) down to least reactive (Cu, Ag, Au). More reactive metals (top of Table J) are stronger reducing agents and have higher oxidation potentials. They react vigorously with water and acids and can displace less reactive metals from their compounds. Less reactive metals (Au, Pt, Ag) are 'noble metals' that resist oxidation—why gold jewelry doesn't tarnish. Regents items use metal reactivity to predict single-replacement reactions and to explain corrosion resistance.

questions.prompt: Process of depositing a thin layer of one metal onto another using an electrolytic cell.
questions.explanation: Electroplating uses an electrolytic cell to deposit a thin layer of one metal onto another object. The object to be plated is the cathode; a piece of the plating metal is the anode; the electrolyte solution contains ions of the plating metal. When current flows, the anode dissolves (oxidation, M → M⁺ + e⁻), and the metal ions reduce onto the cathode (M⁺ + e⁻ → M). Industrial uses: chrome plating on bumpers, gold plating on connectors, silver plating on cutlery, zinc plating (galvanizing) on steel. NYS Regents items use electroplating to identify cathode/anode, electrolyte, and mass changes.

questions.prompt: Slow oxidation of metals by environmental factors, like iron rusting when exposed to water and oxygen.
questions.explanation: Corrosion is the gradual oxidation of metals by the environment, especially atmospheric oxygen and water. Iron rusting is the most common example: 4Fe + 3O₂ → 2Fe₂O₃ (or hydrated, Fe₂O₃·xH₂O = rust). The process is electrochemical: different parts of the iron surface act as anode and cathode, with water and dissolved ions completing the circuit. Salt accelerates corrosion (why salted roads damage cars). Aluminum corrodes too but forms a protective Al₂O₃ layer that stops further oxidation. NYS Regents items use corrosion to illustrate spontaneous oxidation and to introduce protective methods (galvanizing, painting, cathodic protection).

questions.prompt: Practical voltaic cell that stores chemical energy for later conversion to electrical energy.
questions.explanation: A battery is a voltaic cell (or collection of voltaic cells) that stores chemical energy and releases it as electrical energy on demand. Common types: alkaline (Zn anode, MnO₂ cathode, KOH electrolyte; 1.5 V); lead-acid car battery (Pb anode, PbO₂ cathode, H₂SO₄ electrolyte; 2.1 V/cell × 6 cells = 12.6 V); lithium-ion (Li intercalation cathode, graphite anode, organic electrolyte; 3.7 V); zinc-carbon. The first practical battery was Volta's 1800 voltaic pile. Rechargeable batteries reverse the chemistry by applying external voltage (electrolytic cell mode). NYS Regents items use batteries to apply voltaic-cell concepts to everyday devices.

questions.prompt: Process of using electricity to drive a non-spontaneous chemical reaction; how aluminum is produced and how water is split.
questions.explanation: Electrolysis is the use of electricity to drive a non-spontaneous redox reaction. The setup is an electrolytic cell with two electrodes in an electrolyte, connected to an external power source. Industrial applications include: production of aluminum from Al₂O₃ (Hall-Héroult process, 1886); electrolysis of water to produce H₂ and O₂; production of chlorine and NaOH from brine (chlor-alkali process); refining of copper. Michael Faraday formulated the quantitative laws of electrolysis in 1834. NYS Regents items use electrolysis to identify how electrical energy is converted into chemical change and to contrast it with voltaic-cell operation.

questions.prompt: Process of coating steel with a layer of zinc to prevent rusting; the zinc oxidizes preferentially as a sacrificial anode.
questions.explanation: Galvanization is the process of coating iron or steel with zinc to prevent corrosion. Even when the zinc coating is scratched, the exposed steel does not rust because zinc, being above iron in NYS Reference Table J, oxidizes preferentially (sacrificial anode protection). Galvanized steel is used in gutters, roofing, fencing, and outdoor structures. The process can be done by hot-dipping (immersing steel in molten zinc) or by electroplating (zinc deposited electrolytically). The term comes from Luigi Galvani, the Italian physician whose 18th-century experiments on frog legs led to the discovery of bioelectricity. NYS Regents items use galvanization to illustrate cathodic protection via the activity series.

questions.prompt: Saturated hydrocarbon containing only single C-C bonds; general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂.
questions.explanation: An alkane is a saturated hydrocarbon containing only single bonds between carbons. The general formula is CₙH₂ₙ₊₂. The simplest alkane is methane (CH₄); subsequent members are ethane (C₂H₆), propane (C₃H₈), butane (C₄H₁₀), and so on. NYS Reference Table P ('Organic Prefixes') lists prefixes for 10 carbons (meth-, eth-, prop-, but-, pent-, hex-, hept-, oct-, non-, dec-). NYS Reference Table Q ('Homologous Series of Hydrocarbons') gives the alkane general formula and examples. Alkanes are 'saturated' because each carbon is bonded to as many hydrogens as possible. Regents items use Tables P and Q for naming, formulas, and reactivity.

questions.prompt: Unsaturated hydrocarbon containing at least one C=C double bond; general formula CₙH₂ₙ.
questions.explanation: An alkene is an unsaturated hydrocarbon containing at least one carbon-carbon double bond (C=C). The general formula is CₙH₂ₙ. The simplest alkene is ethene/ethylene (C₂H₄), followed by propene (C₃H₆) and butene (C₄H₈). NYS Reference Table Q lists the alkene general formula. Ethene (ethylene) is the most produced industrial organic compound, used to make polyethylene plastic. Alkenes undergo addition reactions because the C=C double bond can break to allow new atoms to attach. Regents items use Table Q to identify alkenes and distinguish them from alkanes by the C=C double bond.

questions.prompt: Unsaturated hydrocarbon containing at least one C≡C triple bond; general formula CₙH₂ₙ₋₂.
questions.explanation: An alkyne is an unsaturated hydrocarbon containing at least one carbon-carbon triple bond (C≡C). The general formula is CₙH₂ₙ₋₂. The simplest alkyne is ethyne/acetylene (C₂H₂), used in welding torches because its combustion produces very high temperatures (~3,500°C). Subsequent alkynes are propyne (C₃H₄), butyne (C₄H₆). NYS Reference Table Q lists the alkyne general formula. The triple bond makes alkynes highly reactive in addition reactions—two pi bonds can break to add 2 hydrogen molecules. Regents items use Table Q to identify alkynes and the C≡C triple bond.

questions.prompt: Compound containing a benzene ring (C₆H₆) or similar planar ring with delocalized pi electrons.
questions.explanation: Aromatic compounds contain a benzene ring (C₆H₆) or a similar planar ring with conjugated pi electrons. Benzene's six carbons are bonded in a hexagonal ring with alternating single and double bonds—but in reality the electrons are delocalized equally around the ring (resonance), making all bond lengths equal. August Kekulé proposed the cyclic structure in 1865. Aromatic compounds include toluene (methylbenzene), styrene, naphthalene (mothballs), and aspirin. Benzene is more stable than expected for a triene, behaving more like an alkane than an alkene. NYS Regents items use aromatic compounds in advanced organic questions, with the benzene ring as the key identifier.

questions.prompt: Compound that has the same molecular formula as another compound but a different structure.
questions.explanation: Isomers are compounds that share the same molecular formula but differ in structural arrangement of atoms. Structural isomers differ in which atoms are connected: n-butane (CH₃-CH₂-CH₂-CH₃) and 2-methylpropane (isobutane) are isomers of C₄H₁₀. Stereoisomers (cis/trans, optical) differ in spatial arrangement. Isomers have different physical and chemical properties despite identical molecular formulas: pentane boils at 36°C; neopentane boils at 9.5°C. The number of possible isomers grows rapidly with carbon count: C₅H₁₂ has 3 isomers; C₁₀H₂₂ has 75. NYS Regents items test isomer identification and the distinction from identical compounds.

questions.prompt: Organic compound containing a hydroxyl (-OH) functional group; names end in -ol.
questions.explanation: An alcohol is an organic compound containing a hydroxyl (-OH) group bonded to a carbon. The general structure is R-OH. Examples: methanol (CH₃OH, wood alcohol), ethanol (C₂H₅OH, drinking alcohol), isopropanol (rubbing alcohol). NYS Reference Table R ('Organic Functional Groups') lists -OH as the alcohol functional group with names ending in -ol. Alcohols are more polar than alkanes due to the -OH group, can form hydrogen bonds, and have higher boiling points. They are key intermediates in organic synthesis—oxidized to aldehydes/ketones, esterified with acids. Regents items use Table R to identify alcohols.

questions.prompt: Organic compound containing a carboxyl (-COOH) functional group; reacts with bases like an Arrhenius acid.
questions.explanation: An organic acid (carboxylic acid) contains the carboxyl group (-COOH), which is a -C(=O)-OH combination. The H in -OH is acidic and can be released, making organic acids weak Arrhenius acids. Examples include acetic acid (CH₃COOH, in vinegar at ~5%), formic acid (HCOOH, in ant stings), and citric acid (in citrus fruits). NYS Reference Table R lists the carboxyl group with names ending in -oic acid. Organic acids react with bases to form salts and water (neutralization) and with alcohols to form esters (esterification). Regents items use Table R to identify organic acids by their -COOH group and naming.

questions.prompt: Organic compound formed by the reaction of an alcohol and an organic acid; often has a fruity smell.
questions.explanation: An ester is an organic compound with the functional group -COO- (acid carbonyl + ester oxygen), formed when an organic acid reacts with an alcohol. The reaction (esterification) eliminates water: R-COOH + R'-OH → R-COO-R' + H₂O. NYS Reference Table R lists the ester functional group with names like ethyl ethanoate (ethyl acetate). Many esters have characteristic fruity smells: ethyl butanoate smells like pineapple; isoamyl acetate smells like banana; methyl salicylate smells like wintergreen. Esters are used as flavorings, fragrances, and solvents. Regents items use Table R to identify esters and Table P/R for naming.

questions.prompt: Organic compound with the -CHO functional group; formaldehyde is the simplest example.
questions.explanation: An aldehyde contains the functional group -CHO (carbonyl C=O with H attached to that carbon)—the C=O sits at the end of a carbon chain. Names end in -al. Examples: methanal/formaldehyde (HCHO, in embalming fluid), ethanal/acetaldehyde (CH₃CHO, a metabolite of ethanol). NYS Reference Table R lists -CHO with names ending in -al. Aldehydes are formed by mild oxidation of primary alcohols; further oxidation gives organic acids. Many natural fragrances (cinnamaldehyde in cinnamon, benzaldehyde in almonds) are aldehydes. Regents items use Table R to distinguish aldehydes (-CHO at chain end) from ketones (-CO- in middle).

questions.prompt: Organic compound with a carbonyl group (C=O) bonded to two carbon atoms; acetone is the simplest example.
questions.explanation: A ketone contains a carbonyl group (C=O) bonded to two carbon atoms—the C=O is in the middle of the chain, not at the end. Names end in -one. The simplest ketone is propanone/acetone (CH₃-CO-CH₃), used as a solvent (nail polish remover). NYS Reference Table R lists ketones with names ending in -one. Ketones are formed by oxidation of secondary alcohols. They are less reactive than aldehydes because both adjacent C-C bonds limit further oxidation. Many sugars (fructose, ribose in ketone form) and steroid hormones contain ketone groups. Regents items use Table R to distinguish ketones from aldehydes.

questions.prompt: Family of organic compounds with the same functional group and a regular pattern of increasing carbon count, like the alkanes.
questions.explanation: A homologous series is a family of organic compounds that share the same functional group and differ from each other by a -CH₂- unit. The alkanes form one of the most well-known homologous series: CH₄, C₂H₆, C₃H₈, C₄H₁₀, each differing by CH₂. Within a series, physical properties change in a regular pattern: boiling point generally rises with carbon count (London dispersion forces grow); reactivity changes systematically; all members share similar chemistry. NYS Reference Table Q lists the alkane, alkene, and alkyne homologous series. Regents items use Tables P, Q, and R to navigate organic naming and homologous-series relationships.

questions.prompt: Systematic naming method developed by the International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry; gives every organic compound a unique name.
questions.explanation: IUPAC nomenclature (International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry) provides a systematic way to name organic compounds. Rules: (1) identify the longest carbon chain containing the principal functional group; (2) number from the end giving substituents the lowest locants; (3) use prefixes from NYS Reference Table P (meth-, eth-, etc.) for chain length; (4) use suffix indicating the family (-ane, -ene, -yne, -ol, -al, -one, -oic acid) from Reference Table R. For example, CH₃-CH(OH)-CH₃ is propan-2-ol. Regents items test IUPAC naming for simple alkanes through alcohols and carboxylic acids using Tables P, Q, and R.

questions.prompt: Drawing showing every bond between atoms in an organic molecule; reveals geometry and connectivity.
questions.explanation: A structural formula shows all atoms and all bonds in an organic molecule, making connectivity explicit. For propane: H-C(H)(H)-C(H)(H)-C(H)(H)-H, with all bonds shown. Structural formulas can be drawn fully (every H shown) or as line-angle/skeletal formulas (carbons at vertices, hydrogens implied). They reveal whether a compound is a straight chain or branched, where double/triple bonds occur, and where functional groups sit. NYS Regents items use structural formulas to identify functional groups, count carbons, identify isomers, and write balanced organic reactions. Compare with condensed and molecular formulas, which show less detail.

questions.prompt: Shortened representation of an organic molecule, like CH₃CH₂CH₃ for propane, without explicit bond lines.
questions.explanation: A condensed formula groups each carbon with its attached hydrogens to compactly show structure without drawing all bonds. Propane = CH₃CH₂CH₃ or CH₃-CH₂-CH₃. Butane = CH₃CH₂CH₂CH₃ or CH₃(CH₂)₂CH₃. Ethanol = CH₃CH₂OH or C₂H₅OH. Functional groups can be shown explicitly: acetic acid is CH₃COOH or CH₃COOH (showing -COOH at the end). Branched molecules use parentheses: isobutane is CH₃CH(CH₃)CH₃ or (CH₃)₃CH. Condensed formulas are more compact than structural formulas but reveal more than molecular formulas. NYS Regents items use them when full structures aren't needed but molecular formulas don't show enough.

questions.prompt: Simple formula stating only the type and count of atoms in a molecule, like C₂H₆O for ethanol.
questions.explanation: A molecular formula gives only the chemical composition: the type and count of each atom in a molecule, without showing structure. Ethanol's molecular formula C₂H₆O could represent ethanol (CH₃CH₂OH) or its isomer dimethyl ether (CH₃OCH₃)—both have 2 C, 6 H, 1 O but very different structures. Glucose is C₆H₁₂O₆; benzene is C₆H₆; ethane is C₂H₆. Molecular formulas are the most compact representation but reveal nothing about isomers or functional groups. Empirical formulas reduce molecular formulas to lowest whole-number ratios (glucose CH₂O). NYS Regents items use molecular formulas to identify isomers and to apply general formulas (e.g., CₙH₂ₙ₊₂ for alkanes).

questions.prompt: Reaction in which an atom or group on a saturated hydrocarbon is replaced by another atom or group.
questions.explanation: A substitution reaction replaces one atom or group in a molecule with another. The classic substitution reaction is the halogenation of an alkane: CH₄ + Cl₂ → CH₃Cl + HCl (a hydrogen is replaced by chlorine). Substitution requires UV light or heat to initiate the radical mechanism. Alkanes are saturated and typically undergo substitution rather than addition. NYS Regents items use substitution to contrast with addition reactions: substitution occurs in alkanes and aromatic compounds, where the carbon skeleton already has maximum bonds; addition occurs in alkenes/alkynes, where new atoms attach across multiple bonds.

questions.prompt: Reaction in which atoms are added across a double or triple bond, converting an unsaturated compound to a more saturated one.
questions.explanation: An addition reaction adds atoms across a multiple bond, converting an unsaturated compound (alkene or alkyne) to a more saturated one. Examples: hydrogenation (alkene + H₂ → alkane, with Pt or Ni catalyst), halogenation (alkene + Br₂ → dibromoalkane—turns brown bromine colorless, a test for unsaturation), hydration (alkene + H₂O → alcohol with acid catalyst). Addition reactions are the characteristic chemistry of alkenes and alkynes, contrasting with substitution in alkanes. The bromine-water test (addition of Br₂ to alkenes/alkynes decolorizes the brown solution) distinguishes saturated from unsaturated hydrocarbons. NYS Regents items use addition reactions in organic synthesis problems.

questions.prompt: Reaction between an alcohol and an organic acid that produces an ester and water.
questions.explanation: Esterification is the reaction of an alcohol with an organic acid to produce an ester and water: R-COOH + R'-OH → R-COO-R' + H₂O. The reaction is catalyzed by concentrated H₂SO₄ and is reversible (Fischer esterification, 1895). Many natural and synthetic esters are produced this way: methyl salicylate (wintergreen flavor, from methanol + salicylic acid), ethyl ethanoate (used as solvent), and various fruit-fragrance esters. Esters are sweetly aromatic—why fresh fruits smell good. Aspirin is made by an analogous reaction (salicylic acid + acetic anhydride). NYS Regents items use esterification on Table R products and reactions, often paired with the ester functional group.

questions.prompt: Anaerobic biological process in which yeast converts sugars to ethanol and carbon dioxide.
