questions.explanation: Fermentation is an anaerobic (no oxygen) biological process in which yeast cells convert sugars (typically glucose) to ethanol and carbon dioxide: C₆H₁₂O₆ → 2C₂H₅OH + 2CO₂. The reaction is catalyzed by enzymes in the yeast (especially zymase). Fermentation has been used since prehistoric times to produce alcoholic beverages (beer, wine), to leaven bread (CO₂ makes dough rise), and now to make biofuel ethanol from corn or sugarcane. Louis Pasteur in the 1850s-60s definitively showed that fermentation requires living yeast cells. NYS Regents items use fermentation as a textbook example of an organic reaction producing ethanol from sugar.

questions.prompt: Reaction of a fat or oil with a strong base to produce soap and glycerol.
questions.explanation: Saponification is the hydrolysis of a fat or oil (triglyceride ester) with a strong base (typically NaOH or KOH) to produce soap (a long-chain carboxylate salt) and glycerol. The reaction has been used for thousands of years to make soap from animal fat (tallow) and lye. Soap molecules have a polar carboxylate head (interacts with water) and a long nonpolar hydrocarbon tail (dissolves grease), letting them clean by emulsifying dirt and oil into water. Hard-water soap problems come from Ca²⁺/Mg²⁺ precipitating soap as scum. NYS Regents items use saponification as a third major organic reaction alongside esterification and fermentation.

questions.prompt: Very large molecule made up of many repeating units, like polyethylene or DNA.
questions.explanation: A polymer is a large molecule consisting of many repeating subunits (monomers) covalently bonded together. Synthetic polymers include polyethylene (used for plastic bags), polystyrene (foam packaging), nylon (clothing), PVC (pipes), and Teflon. Natural polymers include cellulose (plant cell walls), proteins (amino acid polymers), DNA, and rubber (isoprene polymer). Polymer chains can be linear, branched, or cross-linked. Properties (strength, flexibility, melting point) depend on monomer choice, chain length, and intermolecular forces between chains. NYS Regents items use polymer concepts to introduce macromolecules and to connect to organic functional groups in monomers.

questions.prompt: Small molecule that combines with others of the same kind to form a polymer.
questions.explanation: A monomer is a small molecule that bonds repeatedly with other monomers to form a polymer. Examples: ethylene (C₂H₄) is the monomer of polyethylene; styrene is the monomer of polystyrene; glucose is the monomer of starch and cellulose; amino acids are the monomers of proteins; nucleotides are the monomers of DNA. Polymerization links monomers via covalent bonds. Addition polymers (polyethylene, PVC) form when alkene monomers' double bonds break and link end-to-end. Condensation polymers (nylon, polyester, DNA, proteins) form with elimination of small molecules (often water). NYS Regents items use monomer/polymer relationships to explain how plastics and biomolecules are built.

questions.prompt: Process in which monomers containing C=C double bonds join end-to-end to form a polymer, with no by-product.
questions.explanation: Addition polymerization is a chemical process in which unsaturated monomers (alkenes) join end-to-end through breaking of C=C double bonds, with no atoms removed or by-product formed. Polyethylene is made from ethylene monomers: nCH₂=CH₂ → -(CH₂-CH₂)ₙ-. The double bond breaks; the resulting single electrons pair with electrons from adjacent monomers to form new C-C single bonds. Common addition polymers include polyethylene (plastic bags), polypropylene (containers), polystyrene (foam), and Teflon (nonstick coatings). NYS Regents items use addition polymerization with simple alkene monomers and contrast it with condensation polymerization (which produces a small-molecule by-product like water).

questions.prompt: Energy resource formed from ancient organic matter over millions of years; primary source of hydrocarbons used today.
questions.explanation: Fossil fuels are hydrocarbon-rich energy resources formed from ancient organic matter (plants, algae, animals) buried and subjected to heat and pressure for millions of years. The three main types are coal (mostly carbon, from plants), petroleum/crude oil (liquid mix of hydrocarbons from marine organisms), and natural gas (mostly methane CH₄). They are non-renewable on human timescales and produce CO₂ when burned, contributing to climate change. Fractional distillation of crude oil separates it into gasoline, kerosene, diesel, and heavy oils based on boiling-point ranges. NYS Regents items use fossil fuels to contextualize organic chemistry and combustion reactions.

questions.prompt: Reaction of a hydrocarbon with oxygen producing carbon dioxide, water, and energy; powers cars, stoves, and power plants.
questions.explanation: Combustion is the reaction of a hydrocarbon (or other fuel) with O₂ to produce CO₂, H₂O, and heat: CₓHᵧ + O₂ → xCO₂ + (y/2)H₂O + energy. Complete combustion produces CO₂ and H₂O; incomplete combustion (insufficient O₂) produces CO (poisonous) and soot. Methane combustion: CH₄ + 2O₂ → CO₂ + 2H₂O + 891 kJ. Combustion is highly exothermic (ΔH << 0) and is the basis of nearly all transportation and electricity generation today. NYS Reference Table I lists heats of combustion for some fuels. Regents items use combustion to test balanced equations, to identify exothermic reactions, and to connect organic chemistry to energy production.

questions.prompt: Heavy positively charged emission consisting of 2 protons and 2 neutrons; a helium-4 nucleus.
questions.explanation: An alpha particle (⁴₂He or α) consists of 2 protons + 2 neutrons—identical to a helium-4 nucleus. With charge +2 and high mass (4 amu), alpha particles travel slowly and are stopped by a sheet of paper or skin. NYS Reference Table O symbolizes alpha as ⁴₂α or ⁴₂He. Alpha emission is common in heavy radioactive nuclei: ²³⁸U → ²³⁴Th + ⁴₂He decreases mass by 4 and atomic number by 2. Ernest Rutherford named alpha radiation in 1899 and identified it as helium nuclei in 1908. Inside the body (ingested or inhaled), alpha emitters can be very dangerous because they deposit energy locally. Regents items use Table O to balance alpha-decay equations.

questions.prompt: High-speed electron emitted from the nucleus when a neutron converts to a proton; symbol ⁰₋₁e.
questions.explanation: A beta particle (β⁻ or ⁰₋₁e) is a high-speed electron emitted from the nucleus when a neutron converts to a proton (n → p + e⁻ + ν̄). Mass is essentially zero (1/1836 amu); charge is −1. Beta particles are more penetrating than alpha particles (stopped by aluminum or thick clothing) and have moderate ionization. Beta emission increases atomic number by 1 without changing mass number: ¹⁴₆C → ¹⁴₇N + ⁰₋₁e. NYS Reference Table O lists the beta symbol. Carbon-14 dating uses beta decay (half-life 5715 years). Regents items use Table O for beta-decay equations; the daughter element has Z+1 compared to the parent.

questions.prompt: High-energy electromagnetic radiation emitted from the nucleus; symbol γ.
questions.explanation: A gamma ray (γ) is a high-energy electromagnetic photon emitted from an excited nucleus, with no mass and no charge. Gamma rays are the most penetrating type of radiation—they require thick lead or concrete shielding to absorb. They often accompany alpha or beta decay as the daughter nucleus drops from an excited state to ground state. Because gamma rays have no mass or charge, they don't change the parent nuclide's identity, but they release significant energy (often in MeV). Cobalt-60 (used in cancer radiotherapy) emits both beta and gamma. NYS Reference Table O symbolizes gamma. Regents items use gamma in nuclear equations and shielding questions.

questions.prompt: Type of radioactive emission in which the nucleus releases a free neutron with no charge.
questions.explanation: Neutron emission releases a free neutron (¹₀n) from an unstable nucleus, decreasing mass number by 1 without changing atomic number: ⁵Be → ⁴Be + ¹₀n (hypothetical). Neutron emission is much less common than alpha, beta, or gamma decay. It can occur in highly neutron-rich isotopes and is significant in nuclear fission, where uranium-235 absorbs a neutron and produces 2-3 free neutrons that can sustain a chain reaction. Free neutrons are penetrating because they have no charge—they slip through electron clouds and interact only with nuclei. NYS Reference Table O lists the neutron symbol. Regents items occasionally use neutron emission in equation balancing.

questions.prompt: Unstable isotope that emits radiation; carbon-14 and uranium-238 are examples.
questions.explanation: A radioisotope (or radionuclide) is an unstable isotope of an element that spontaneously emits radiation to reach a more stable configuration. NYS Reference Table N ('Selected Radioisotopes') lists common radioisotopes with their half-lives and decay modes: ¹⁴C (5715 y, β), ²³⁸U (4.47 × 10⁹ y, α), ¹³¹I (8.021 d, β), ⁹⁰Sr (29.1 y, β), ²²²Rn (3.823 d, α). Radioisotopes are used in medicine (radiation therapy, PET scans), dating (carbon-14 for organic remains, uranium-lead for rocks), industrial gauges, and energy production. NYS Regents items use Table N to identify decay modes, half-lives, and resulting daughter nuclides.

questions.prompt: Region on a graph of neutrons vs. protons where stable nuclei lie; outside this region, nuclei are radioactive.
questions.explanation: The band (or belt) of stability is the region on a plot of neutrons (N) vs. protons (Z) where stable nuclei cluster. For low-Z elements, the band runs along N ≈ Z (carbon-12 has 6n, 6p). For higher-Z elements, the band curves above N = Z—stable heavy nuclei have more neutrons than protons (e.g., gold-197 has 79p, 118n). Nuclei above the band have too many neutrons and tend to undergo beta decay (n → p + e⁻); nuclei below the band have too many protons and tend to undergo positron emission or electron capture. Beyond Z = 83 (bismuth), all isotopes are radioactive. NYS Regents items use the band of stability conceptually to predict decay modes.

questions.prompt: Change in the nucleus of an atom that converts one element into another; can be natural or artificial.
questions.explanation: Transmutation is the conversion of one element into another by changing the number of protons in the nucleus. Natural transmutation occurs in radioactive decay: ²³⁸U → ²³⁴Th + α turns uranium into thorium. Artificial transmutation occurs when a nucleus is bombarded with particles: Rutherford's famous 1919 experiment (¹⁴N + ⁴α → ¹⁷O + ¹p) was the first artificial transmutation, transmuting nitrogen into oxygen. Modern particle accelerators routinely transmute elements; the synthetic elements (Z > 92) all come from particle-induced transmutation. NYS Regents items use transmutation in artificial nuclear-reaction problems requiring equation balancing on Table O.

questions.prompt: Sequence of nuclear decays starting from one unstable isotope and ending with a stable nuclide.
questions.explanation: A decay series (or decay chain) is a sequence of radioactive decays leading from an unstable parent isotope through a series of unstable intermediates to a stable end product. The uranium-238 series begins with ²³⁸U and ends at ²⁰⁶Pb after 14 decays (8 alpha + 6 beta). The thorium-232 series ends at ²⁰⁸Pb; the uranium-235 series ends at ²⁰⁷Pb. Each step in a decay series has its own half-life; some intermediates last seconds, others millions of years. Decay series are how heavy elements naturally exist on Earth despite billions of years of decay. NYS Regents items may reference decay chains conceptually.

questions.prompt: Balanced statement showing the parent nucleus, daughter nucleus, and emitted particles in a nuclear change.
questions.explanation: A nuclear equation shows reactants, products, and emitted particles for a nuclear change. Like chemical equations, nuclear equations must balance—but instead of atom counts, mass numbers and atomic numbers must balance separately. For alpha decay of ²³⁸U: ²³⁸₉₂U → ²³⁴₉₀Th + ⁴₂He (mass: 238 = 234 + 4; charge: 92 = 90 + 2). For beta decay of ¹⁴C: ¹⁴₆C → ¹⁴₇N + ⁰₋₁e (mass: 14 = 14 + 0; charge: 6 = 7 + (−1)). NYS Reference Table O provides the symbols for particles needed to balance nuclear equations. Regents items routinely require students to fill in missing nuclides in nuclear equations.

questions.prompt: Time required for half the atoms in a radioactive sample to decay; listed on Reference Table N for many isotopes.
questions.explanation: Half-life (t½) is the time required for half the radioactive atoms in a sample to decay. After 1 half-life, 1/2 remains; after 2 half-lives, 1/4 remains; after 3, 1/8; after n half-lives, (1/2)ⁿ. Half-lives vary enormously: ²¹⁴Po has t½ = 164 μs; ²³⁸U has t½ = 4.47 × 10⁹ years. NYS Reference Table N lists half-lives for common isotopes including ¹⁴C (5715 y), ¹³¹I (8.021 d), ³⁷K (1.23 s). Half-life is constant for each isotope, unaffected by temperature, pressure, or chemical state. Regents items use Table N to calculate remaining mass after a given number of half-lives.

questions.prompt: Graph plotting remaining radioactive mass versus time; shows the exponential decline of an isotope.
questions.explanation: A decay curve plots remaining mass (or fraction of original) on the y-axis versus time on the x-axis. The curve is exponential: M = M₀ · (1/2)^(t/t½). Each half-life cuts the y-value in half. The curve approaches but never reaches zero. Reading a decay curve: pick a y-value, drop down to find the time; pick a time, look up to find the remaining fraction. Decay curves are essential tools for radiocarbon dating (matching ¹⁴C decay to age) and for predicting radioactive waste safety timelines. NYS Regents items provide decay-curve graphs and ask students to determine half-life from the graph or to read remaining mass.

questions.prompt: Technique using the half-life of carbon-14 (5715 years) to estimate the age of organic remains up to about 50,000 years old.
questions.explanation: Carbon dating (radiocarbon dating) uses the decay of carbon-14 to estimate the age of organic remains. Living organisms maintain a constant ratio of ¹⁴C/¹²C by exchanging carbon with the atmosphere; when they die, ¹⁴C decays with a half-life of 5715 years (NYS Reference Table N) without replenishment. Measuring the ¹⁴C/¹²C ratio in a sample compared to a modern reference gives the age. Useful for samples up to about 50,000 years old (about 10 half-lives). Willard Libby developed the technique in 1949 (Nobel Prize 1960). NYS Regents items use carbon dating as a textbook application of half-life, often with simple half-life count problems.

questions.prompt: Mathematical proportion of a radioactive sample still present after a given amount of time; equals (1/2)ⁿ where n is the number of half-lives.
questions.explanation: The remaining fraction of a radioactive sample after n half-lives is (1/2)ⁿ. After 1 half-life, 1/2 = 50% remains; after 2, 1/4 = 25%; after 3, 1/8 = 12.5%; after 4, 1/16 = 6.25%; after 5, 1/32 = 3.125%. The amount that has decayed is (1 − (1/2)ⁿ). For a problem: 'How much of a 100 g sample of ¹³¹I remains after 24 days?' Since ¹³¹I has t½ = 8.021 d (NYS Table N), 24 days ≈ 3 half-lives, so remaining = 100 × (1/2)³ = 12.5 g. NYS Regents items routinely test these calculations with values from Table N or with a decay graph.

questions.prompt: Nickname for the use of radioactive decay rates as a precise method to determine ages of ancient materials.
questions.explanation: A radioactive 'clock' uses the constant decay rate of a radioisotope to determine the age of a sample. Different isotopes serve different age ranges: ¹⁴C (t½ 5715 y) for organic materials up to ~50,000 years; ⁴⁰K (t½ 1.25 × 10⁹ y) for rocks billions of years old (potassium-argon dating); ²³⁸U/²³⁵U for the oldest rocks and meteorites. The technique measures the parent/daughter isotope ratio, calculates how many half-lives have passed, and converts to absolute age. NYS Reference Table N lists half-lives. Radioactive dating gave the first reliable estimates of Earth's age (~4.54 billion years). Regents items reference this concept along with carbon-14 dating.

questions.prompt: Splitting of a heavy nucleus into lighter ones, releasing energy; powers nuclear reactors and atomic bombs.
questions.explanation: Nuclear fission is the splitting of a heavy nucleus into two or three lighter nuclei, accompanied by release of 2-3 neutrons and a tremendous amount of energy (about 200 MeV per uranium-235 fission). The classic example: ¹n + ²³⁵U → ¹⁴¹Ba + ⁹²Kr + 3 ¹n. The released neutrons can trigger further fission, producing a chain reaction. Otto Hahn and Fritz Strassmann discovered fission in 1938 (Lise Meitner interpreted the chemistry). Fission powers commercial nuclear reactors and atomic weapons. The mass defect (small mass loss) is converted to energy via E = mc². NYS Regents items use fission to illustrate nuclear-energy concepts and mass-energy conversion.

questions.prompt: Joining of light nuclei to form a heavier one, releasing enormous energy; powers the sun.
questions.explanation: Nuclear fusion is the joining of two light nuclei to form a heavier one, releasing energy because the products have less mass than the reactants (E = mc²). The sun is powered by hydrogen fusion: 4 ¹H → ⁴He + 2 ⁰₊₁e + neutrinos + energy. On Earth, the most explored fusion reaction is ²H + ³H → ⁴He + ¹n + 17.6 MeV (deuterium-tritium fusion). Fusion requires extreme temperatures (~10⁸ K) to overcome electrostatic repulsion between positive nuclei. Hydrogen bombs use fusion; tokamak reactors aim to harness it for clean energy. Fusion produces less radioactive waste than fission. NYS Regents items contrast fusion with fission as the two major mass-energy conversion processes.

questions.prompt: Self-sustaining sequence of nuclear fissions, in which neutrons from one fission trigger more fissions.
questions.explanation: A chain reaction is a self-sustaining series of nuclear fissions, where neutrons released by one fission event cause additional fissions in nearby nuclei. Each ²³⁵U fission releases 2-3 neutrons that can trigger further fissions. If on average exactly one neutron from each fission causes another, the reaction is critical and self-sustaining (used in reactors). If more than one, supercritical (used in bombs—uncontrolled exponential growth). If less than one, subcritical (dies out). Enrico Fermi achieved the first controlled chain reaction at Chicago Pile-1 in 1942. NYS Regents items use chain reactions to explain how fission reactors and weapons work.

questions.prompt: Minimum amount of fissile material needed to sustain a nuclear chain reaction.
questions.explanation: Critical mass is the minimum amount of fissile material (²³⁵U, ²³⁹Pu) needed to sustain a nuclear chain reaction—enough material that, on average, at least one neutron from each fission triggers another. For ²³⁵U metal at normal density, critical mass is about 52 kg (a sphere ~17 cm across). Critical mass depends on density, geometry (sphere = lowest), and reflector presence (which bounces escaping neutrons back). In reactors, critical mass plus control rods (which absorb neutrons) maintains a steady reaction. In atomic bombs, two subcritical pieces are slammed together to exceed critical mass for a brief, devastating chain reaction. NYS Regents items use critical mass conceptually.

questions.prompt: Conversion of a tiny amount of mass into a huge amount of energy according to Einstein's equation E = mc².
questions.explanation: Mass-energy conversion describes the equivalence of mass and energy: Einstein's 1905 equation E = mc² states that energy equals mass times the speed of light squared (c ≈ 3 × 10⁸ m/s). Because c² is enormous, even a tiny mass conversion releases vast energy. In nuclear fission of ²³⁵U, about 0.1% of the reactant mass is converted to energy (~200 MeV per fission). In hydrogen fusion in the sun, about 0.7% of mass is converted. NYS Reference Table T contains the equation E = mc². Regents items use mass-energy conversion to explain why nuclear reactions release millions of times more energy per kilogram than chemical reactions.

questions.prompt: Radioactive isotope used to follow the movement of a substance through a system; used in medical imaging and biological research.
questions.explanation: A tracer isotope is a radioactive isotope used to track the path of a substance through a biological, chemical, or physical system. The isotope chemically behaves like its stable counterpart but can be detected by its radiation. Medical examples: technetium-99m (the most common medical tracer, used in bone, heart, and brain scans, t½ 6 h); iodine-131 (thyroid imaging and treatment, t½ 8.021 d, NYS Table N); fluorine-18 (PET scans). George de Hevesy pioneered radiotracer techniques in the 1910s (Nobel Prize 1943). NYS Regents items use tracers as a beneficial application of radioactivity and reference Table N for medical isotopes.

questions.prompt: Medical treatment using high-energy radiation to destroy cancer cells.
questions.explanation: Radiation therapy (radiotherapy) uses high-energy radiation—often gamma rays from cobalt-60 or X-rays from linear accelerators, or charged particles like protons—to damage DNA in cancer cells and stop them from dividing. Cobalt-60 (t½ 5.27 y, NYS Reference Table N) is a common gamma source. Treatment is delivered in fractions over weeks to maximize cancer-cell death while letting normal tissue recover. Brachytherapy uses sealed radioactive seeds implanted near the tumor (e.g., iodine-125 for prostate cancer). Marie Curie's discovery of radium in 1898 enabled the earliest radiation therapy. NYS Regents items use radiation therapy as a beneficial medical application of radioactivity.

questions.prompt: Industrial facility that uses controlled nuclear fission to generate electricity.
questions.explanation: A nuclear power plant uses controlled fission of uranium-235 (or plutonium-239) to heat water, generate steam, and drive turbines that produce electricity. Components: fuel rods (enriched uranium), moderator (water or graphite, slows neutrons), control rods (cadmium or boron, absorb neutrons), coolant (often water), containment building. About 10% of global electricity comes from nuclear power. Major incidents (Chernobyl 1986, Fukushima 2011, Three Mile Island 1979) highlight safety concerns; storage of long-lived radioactive waste is a persistent challenge. The first commercial reactor opened in 1954 (Obninsk, USSR). NYS Regents items use power plants as an application of fission energy.

questions.prompt: Material used to absorb radiation and protect from exposure; lead for gamma, paper for alpha, aluminum for beta.
questions.explanation: Shielding is material used to block or attenuate ionizing radiation. Effective shielding depends on radiation type: alpha particles are stopped by a sheet of paper or skin; beta particles by aluminum foil or thick plastic (~1 cm); gamma rays and X-rays require dense materials like lead (several cm) or concrete (~30 cm thick); neutrons are best blocked by water or plastic containing hydrogen. Medical X-ray technicians wear lead aprons; nuclear-reactor containment vessels use thick concrete + steel. Cosmic-ray shielding for astronauts is a major engineering challenge. NYS Regents items use shielding to demonstrate radiation penetration differences and to apply safety principles.

questions.prompt: Naturally occurring ionizing radiation from cosmic rays, soil, building materials, and food.
questions.explanation: Background radiation is the naturally occurring ionizing radiation everyone experiences, regardless of human activity. Sources: cosmic rays (from space, more intense at high altitude); terrestrial sources (uranium and thorium in soil and rocks); radon gas (²²²Rn, the largest single source for many people); internal radiation from naturally occurring ⁴⁰K and ¹⁴C in the body; medical and dental X-rays; building materials. Average background dose in the US is about 3 mSv/year. Living in Denver or flying frequently adds dose. Background radiation provides essential context: it's the baseline against which artificial radiation exposure must be compared. NYS Regents items use background radiation as a natural-occurrence concept.

questions.prompt: Tally of protons, neutrons, and electrons in a neutral atom or ion; uses the atomic number from the NYS Periodic Table and the mass number.
questions.explanation: Counting subatomic particles is a foundational Regents skill. Number of protons = atomic number (Z, from NYS Periodic Table). Number of neutrons = mass number − atomic number. Number of electrons in a neutral atom = atomic number; in an ion, electrons = protons − charge (so Na⁺ has 11 − 1 = 10 electrons; O²⁻ has 8 + 2 = 10 electrons). For ³⁵₁₇Cl: 17 protons, 18 neutrons (35 − 17), 17 electrons (neutral). The work was made possible by Rutherford's 1911 nucleus discovery and Chadwick's 1932 neutron identification. Regents items routinely test this calculation in isotope and ion questions.

questions.prompt: Computation of an element's atomic mass from the masses and percent abundances of its isotopes.
questions.explanation: Isotope calculation uses the weighted-average formula: atomic mass = Σ(isotope mass × fractional abundance). For chlorine: (34.97 × 0.7577) + (36.97 × 0.2423) ≈ 35.45 amu—matching the value on the NYS Periodic Table. To convert percent to fraction, divide by 100. The atomic mass on the Periodic Table reflects the natural mix of isotopes, which is why most atomic masses are decimals rather than whole numbers. The concept of isotopes was coined by Frederick Soddy in 1913. NYS Regents items provide isotope data and require students to calculate atomic mass and match it to a Periodic Table entry.

questions.prompt: Distribution of electrons across shells of an atom; printed in every Periodic Table box.
questions.explanation: Electron configuration shows how electrons are arranged in shells from the innermost outward, as in 2-8-1 for sodium. NYS Periodic Table boxes display the ground-state configuration for each element. Bohr's 1913 model introduced this shell picture. The configuration determines chemical behavior: valence electrons (outermost shell) are involved in bonding, and group number predicts valence count. Excited-state configurations have an electron promoted to a higher shell (e.g., Na could go from 2-8-1 to 2-7-2). Regents items use electron configuration to identify ground vs. excited states, predict ion charges, and explain the periodic law.

questions.prompt: Pattern of how a property like atomic radius or electronegativity changes across periods and down groups on the Periodic Table.
questions.explanation: A periodic trend describes how an atomic property changes across a row or down a column on the Periodic Table. Key trends, listed on NYS Reference Table S: atomic radius decreases left-to-right across a period and increases down a group; ionization energy increases left-to-right and decreases down; electronegativity increases left-to-right and decreases down (F is highest); metallic character does the opposite. The patterns reflect changes in nuclear charge, shell count, and electron shielding. Mendeleev's 1869 periodic law captured this regularity, refined by Moseley's 1913 atomic-number ordering. Regents items use Table S to compare two elements' properties.

questions.prompt: Process by which an atom becomes a cation by losing electrons or an anion by gaining them, achieving a stable noble-gas configuration.
questions.explanation: Ion formation occurs when an atom gains or loses electrons to achieve a stable (often noble-gas) electron configuration. Metals (low ionization energy) lose electrons to form cations: Na → Na⁺ + e⁻ (achieves Ne configuration). Nonmetals (high electronegativity) gain electrons to form anions: Cl + e⁻ → Cl⁻ (achieves Ar configuration). The octet rule (Lewis 1916) drives most ion formation: atoms tend toward 8 valence electrons. Group number predicts ion charge: Group 1 → +1, Group 2 → +2, Group 17 → −1, Group 16 → −2. NYS Regents items use ion formation to write balanced ionic-compound formulas and to analyze redox reactions.

questions.prompt: Unequal sharing of electrons in a covalent bond, classified using the electronegativity difference on Reference Table S.
questions.explanation: Bond polarity depends on the electronegativity difference (ΔEN) between bonded atoms. Using NYS Reference Table S: ΔEN < 0.5 = nonpolar covalent (H-H, C-H); 0.5 to 1.7 = polar covalent (H-O, H-N); > 1.7 = generally ionic (Na-Cl). Polar covalent bonds have partial charges (δ⁺ and δ⁻). Linus Pauling introduced the electronegativity scale in 1932. Bond polarity is a key piece of the polarity puzzle: a polar bond doesn't automatically make a polar molecule (CO₂ has polar bonds but is nonpolar due to symmetry). NYS Regents items use ΔEN calculations to classify bonds and to predict molecular polarity.

questions.prompt: 3D geometry of a covalent molecule, predicted from the number of electron pairs around the central atom using VSEPR theory.
questions.explanation: Molecular shape (geometry) is determined by VSEPR theory (Valence Shell Electron Pair Repulsion). Electron pairs around a central atom arrange themselves as far apart as possible. Common shapes: 2 pairs → linear (CO₂, 180°); 3 pairs → trigonal planar (BF₃, 120°); 4 pairs → tetrahedral (CH₄, 109.5°); 4 pairs with 1 lone → trigonal pyramidal (NH₃, 107°); 4 pairs with 2 lone → bent (H₂O, 104.5°). Shape plus bond polarity determines molecular polarity: symmetric shapes (linear with equal substituents, tetrahedral with all same) make nonpolar molecules even if bonds are polar. NYS Regents items use VSEPR-predicted shapes for polarity and solubility predictions.

questions.prompt: Attraction between molecules, ranging from weakest (London dispersion) to strongest (hydrogen bonding) — affects boiling point and solubility.
questions.explanation: Intermolecular forces (IMFs) are attractions between molecules, weaker than the covalent bonds within them. In order of increasing strength: London dispersion forces (all molecules, from temporary dipoles, Fritz London 1930); dipole-dipole (polar molecules); hydrogen bonding (H bonded to N, O, or F). Ion-dipole forces are even stronger and explain ionic solubility in water. Stronger IMFs mean higher boiling points: water boils at 100°C (extensive H-bonding) while H₂S boils at −60°C despite similar mass. NYS Regents items use IMFs to explain solubility (like dissolves like), boiling-point trends, and water's anomalous properties.

questions.prompt: Graph showing how temperature changes as heat is added to a substance through solid, liquid, and gas phases — has plateaus at melting and boiling points.
questions.explanation: A phase-change (heating) graph plots temperature on the y-axis versus heat added on the x-axis, showing the five segments of solid heating, melting plateau, liquid heating, boiling plateau, and gas heating. During the two plateaus, temperature stays constant while heat goes into breaking intermolecular forces (potential energy increase rather than kinetic energy). For water, plateaus occur at 0°C and 100°C. Slope of non-plateau segments depends on specific heat (NYS Reference Table B); plateau lengths depend on heats of fusion and vaporization (also Table B). Regents items use heating curves to test energy calculations (q = mCΔT, q = mHf, q = mHv) and to identify phases.

questions.prompt: Mathematical proportion linking pressure, volume, and absolute temperature of an ideal gas; combined gas law on Reference Table T.
questions.explanation: Gas-law relationships connect P, V, and T for ideal gases. The combined gas law (NYS Reference Table T) is P₁V₁/T₁ = P₂V₂/T₂, with T in Kelvin. It reduces to Boyle's law (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂) at constant T; Charles's law (V₁/T₁ = V₂/T₂) at constant P; Gay-Lussac's law (P₁/T₁ = P₂/T₂) at constant V. The ideal gas law PV = nRT incorporates moles. Avogadro's law states equal volumes contain equal numbers of particles—so 22.4 L of any gas at STP contains 6.022 × 10²³ molecules. Robert Boyle (1662), Jacques Charles (c.1787), Amedeo Avogadro (1811), and Joseph Gay-Lussac (1802) each contributed. NYS Regents items use these laws throughout gas problems.

questions.prompt: Conversion between grams of a substance and moles using molar mass; uses 6.022 × 10²³ for particle counts.
questions.explanation: A mole conversion translates between moles, mass, particles, and volume. Key relationships from NYS Reference Table T: moles = mass / gram formula mass; moles × Avogadro's number (6.022 × 10²³) = number of particles; at STP, moles × 22.4 L/mol = volume of gas. The mole is the SI unit of amount of substance, defined as the number of atoms in 12 g of ¹²C (6.022 × 10²³). Avogadro proposed the conceptual basis in 1811; Jean Perrin's 1908 experiments determined the numerical value. NYS Regents items use mole conversions in stoichiometry and concentration problems.

questions.prompt: Calculation determining moles or grams of solute given molarity and volume of solution; uses M = mol/L.
questions.explanation: A molarity calculation uses M = moles of solute / liters of solution (NYS Reference Table T). To find moles of NaCl in 500 mL of 0.40 M solution: moles = M × V = 0.40 × 0.500 = 0.20 mol; mass = 0.20 × 58.5 = 11.7 g. The dilution equation M₁V₁ = M₂V₂ extends this for adding solvent. Molarity calculations underlie every quantitative solution problem on the Regents: preparation, dilution, stoichiometry, and titration. Volume units must be in liters; mass to moles requires the formula mass. NYS Regents items test molarity in concentration problems and titration setups.

questions.prompt: Mole-to-mole proportion derived from coefficients of a balanced chemical equation; the basis of all stoichiometry.
questions.explanation: A stoichiometric ratio is the mole ratio of reactants and products in a balanced chemical equation. For 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O: the H₂:O₂:H₂O ratio is 2:1:2. To find moles of H₂O from 0.30 mol O₂: 0.30 × (2/1) = 0.60 mol H₂O. Coefficient counting is the foundation of stoichiometry. The mass conservation principle (Lavoisier 1789) requires every chemical equation to balance. NYS Regents items use stoichiometric ratios in every quantitative reaction problem: predicting product mass, finding limiting reactant, calculating percent yield, gas-volume relationships.

questions.prompt: Reactant that runs out first in a chemical reaction, determining the maximum amount of product that can form.
questions.explanation: The limiting reactant (or limiting reagent) is the reactant that runs out first in a reaction, capping the amount of product. The other reactant(s) are 'in excess.' To identify the limiting reactant: convert all reactants to moles, divide each by its stoichiometric coefficient, and pick the smallest value. For 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O with 4 mol H₂ and 1 mol O₂: H₂ gives 4/2 = 2, O₂ gives 1/1 = 1, so O₂ is limiting; max H₂O = 2 mol. The limiting-reactant concept ensures students understand that real reactions are constrained by available reactants. NYS Regents items use limiting-reactant problems to test ratio reasoning and product-yield prediction.

questions.prompt: Comparison of actual product mass to the theoretical maximum, expressed as a percentage; measures reaction efficiency.
questions.explanation: Percent yield = (actual yield / theoretical yield) × 100. Theoretical yield is calculated from the limiting reactant using stoichiometry; actual yield is the mass actually obtained in the lab. A reaction producing 8.0 g of product when stoichiometry predicts 10.0 g has 80% yield. Reasons for less than 100%: incomplete reaction, side reactions, loss during transfer/purification, equilibrium constraints, measurement errors. Industrial chemistry aims for high yields to maximize profit. NYS Reference Table T includes the percent-yield formula. Regents items use percent-yield calculations in stoichiometry problems and reagent-economy questions.

questions.prompt: Variable that changes the speed of a chemical reaction; includes temperature, concentration, surface area, catalysts, and pressure (for gases).
questions.explanation: A rate factor is any variable that changes the speed of a chemical reaction. The major rate factors and their effects: increasing temperature speeds rate (more particles exceed activation energy); increasing reactant concentration speeds rate (more collisions per second); increasing surface area speeds rate (more contact); adding a catalyst lowers activation energy and speeds rate without being consumed; increasing pressure speeds gas-phase reactions. All are explained by collision theory: rate increases when collisions become more frequent or more effective. Arrhenius quantified the temperature effect in 1889 with k = A·exp(−Ea/RT). NYS Regents items test which factors affect rate and why.

questions.prompt: Graph plotting potential energy vs. reaction progress; shows activation energy, heat of reaction, and the activated complex.
questions.explanation: A potential energy (PE) diagram (energy diagram) plots PE on the y-axis versus reaction progress on the x-axis. Key features: PE of reactants (left), PE of products (right), activation energy (peak height above reactants), Ea(reverse) (peak height above products), heat of reaction ΔH = products − reactants, and the activated complex at the peak. Exothermic reactions: products below reactants, ΔH < 0. Endothermic reactions: products above reactants, ΔH > 0. Adding a catalyst lowers the peak without changing reactants, products, or ΔH. NYS Regents items test reading PE diagrams to identify Ea, ΔH, and catalyst effects.

questions.prompt: Change in equilibrium position caused by a stress; predicted by Le Chatelier's principle (1884).
questions.explanation: An equilibrium shift occurs when a stress is applied to a system at equilibrium. Le Chatelier's principle (Henry Louis Le Chatelier, 1884) states that the system responds by shifting in the direction that counteracts the stress. Stresses: (1) concentration change → shift away from added substance; (2) temperature change → shift away from added heat (heat is reactant in endothermic, product in exothermic); (3) pressure change (gas reactions only with different mole counts) → shift toward fewer moles when pressure increases. Catalysts do NOT shift equilibrium (they speed both directions equally). NYS Regents items test direction of shift and the reasons.

questions.prompt: Reaction between an acid and a base producing a salt and water; classic acid-base reaction with net ionic H⁺ + OH⁻ → H₂O.
questions.explanation: Acid-base neutralization combines an acid and base to produce salt + water. General: acid + base → salt + water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O. Net ionic: H⁺ + OH⁻ → H₂O (the rest are spectators). Arrhenius (1884) defined acids by H⁺ release and bases by OH⁻ release. Neutralization is highly exothermic (ΔH ≈ −56 kJ/mol of water). Titration is the lab technique for quantitative neutralization using the equation M_A V_A = M_B V_B (NYS Reference Table T). Indicators (Reference Table M) signal endpoint by color change. NYS Regents items use neutralization throughout acid-base chemistry, with Tables K, L, M, and T as supports.

questions.prompt: Movement of electrons from one species to another in an oxidation-reduction reaction; the defining process of redox chemistry.
questions.explanation: Redox transfer is the movement of electrons from one species (oxidized) to another (reduced) in a redox reaction. Oxidation = loss of electrons (LEO); reduction = gain of electrons (GER). For Zn + Cu²⁺ → Zn²⁺ + Cu: Zn loses 2e⁻ (oxidized); Cu²⁺ gains 2e⁻ (reduced). Total e⁻ lost = total e⁻ gained in a balanced equation. Half-reactions track each step separately. NYS Reference Table J (activity series) predicts spontaneity: a metal higher on Table J can displace a lower metal from solution. Voltaic cells (Volta 1800) harness redox to generate electricity; electrolytic cells (Faraday 1834) use electricity to force non-spontaneous redox. NYS Regents items use redox transfer throughout electrochemistry.

questions.prompt: Identification of an alcohol, organic acid, ester, aldehyde, ketone, or other characteristic group within an organic molecule using Reference Table R.
questions.explanation: Functional group identification uses NYS Reference Table R ('Organic Functional Groups'). The R table lists: alcohol (-OH, -ol), organic acid (-COOH, -oic acid), ester (-COO-, -oate), aldehyde (-CHO, -al), ketone (-C(=O)-, -one), amine (-NH₂, -amine), amide (-C(=O)NH₂), halide (-X, halo-), and others. The functional group determines a compound's chemistry: -OH groups can be oxidized to aldehydes/ketones; -COOH groups react with alcohols to form esters (esterification); -COO- groups undergo hydrolysis (saponification). NYS Regents items use Table R to name compounds, identify families, and predict reactions.

questions.prompt: Category of organic reaction such as substitution, addition, esterification, fermentation, saponification, or combustion.
questions.explanation: Major organic reaction types covered in NYS Chemistry Regents: (1) substitution—atom or group replaces another in an alkane (CH₄ + Cl₂ → CH₃Cl + HCl); (2) addition—atoms add across a multiple bond in an alkene/alkyne (CH₂=CH₂ + H₂ → CH₃CH₃); (3) esterification—alcohol + organic acid → ester + water; (4) fermentation—sugars → ethanol + CO₂ via yeast; (5) saponification—fat + base → soap + glycerol; (6) combustion—hydrocarbon + O₂ → CO₂ + H₂O + heat; (7) addition polymerization—alkene monomers → polymer chain. Identifying the reaction type clarifies products and mechanism. NYS Regents items test recognition of these reaction types.

questions.prompt: Repeating-unit relationship between monomers and polymers; key example is polyethylene built from ethene monomers.
questions.explanation: A polymer pattern shows how repeating monomer units assemble into a larger molecule. In addition polymerization: ethene (CH₂=CH₂) double bonds break and link end-to-end: n CH₂=CH₂ → −(CH₂-CH₂)ₙ− = polyethylene. The repeating unit (in parentheses) tells you the monomer. Other patterns: polystyrene from styrene monomers; PVC from vinyl chloride; nylon (condensation polymer) from diamines and diacids with water elimination. Natural polymer patterns: starch from glucose, proteins from amino acids, DNA from nucleotides. NYS Regents items use polymer patterns to identify monomers from a polymer structure and to predict polymer formulas from a given monomer.

questions.prompt: Type of decay (alpha, beta, positron, gamma, or neutron emission) shown in a nuclear equation; balanced using mass and atomic numbers.
questions.explanation: A nuclear decay mode is identified by the emitted particle: alpha (⁴₂He, decreases mass by 4 and atomic number by 2); beta (⁰₋₁e, increases atomic number by 1, same mass); positron (⁰₊₁e, decreases atomic number by 1, same mass); gamma (γ, no mass/charge change); neutron emission (¹₀n, decreases mass by 1, same atomic number). NYS Reference Table O lists particle symbols. NYS Reference Table N tabulates decay modes for selected radioisotopes (¹⁴C beta, ²³⁸U alpha, ¹³¹I beta, etc.). To balance: mass numbers and atomic numbers must each equal on both sides. Regents items use Table N + O to fill in missing nuclides in decay equations.

questions.prompt: Decay graph plotting remaining mass or fraction versus time; reading it gives half-life, age, or fraction remaining.
questions.explanation: A half-life graph plots remaining radioactive mass (or fraction) on the y-axis vs. time on the x-axis. The curve is exponential: each half-life cuts y in half. Reading techniques: locate y = 50% to find one half-life; locate y = 25% for two; y = 12.5% for three. Given time elapsed, divide by half-life (t½) to get number of half-lives n, then compute (1/2)ⁿ × initial = remaining. ¹⁴C half-life is 5715 years (NYS Reference Table N). For example, a 64 g sample after 4 half-lives = 64 × (1/2)⁴ = 4 g remaining. NYS Regents items provide half-life graphs and test all these reading skills, including identification of the isotope from its t½.
